nd.topk regression with nan values
issue_body:
mxnet master contains a regression in the nd.topk operator compared to mxnet v1.2. Likely https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10997 is at fault (but I have not performed an bisection to proof that).

Consider the following:

Behavior in mxnet master:
```
In [1]: a = [np.nan] * 4 + list(range(2500))
    ...: a = mx.nd.array(a)
    ...: print(a)
    ...: for k in range(3,10):
    ...:     print(mx.nd.topk(a, k=k))
    ...:
    ...:
[  nan   nan   nan ... 2497. 2498. 2499.]
<NDArray 2504 @cpu(0)>

[2. 0. 1.]
<NDArray 3 @cpu(0)>

[2. 0. 1. 3.]
<NDArray 4 @cpu(0)>

[2. 4. 0. 1. 3.]
<NDArray 5 @cpu(0)>

[5. 4. 2. 0. 1. 3.]
<NDArray 6 @cpu(0)>

[2. 6. 5. 4. 0. 1. 3.]
<NDArray 7 @cpu(0)>

[ 2.  1. 10.  9.  8.  6.  0.  3.]
<NDArray 8 @cpu(0)>

[ 2.  1. 11. 10.  9.  6.  8.  0.  3.]
<NDArray 9 @cpu(0)>
```

Behavior in mxnet v1.2:
```
In [1]: In [1]: a = [np.nan] * 4 + list(range(2500))
   ...:     ...: a = mx.nd.array(a)
   ...:     ...: print(a)
   ...:     ...: for k in range(3,10):
   ...:     ...:     print(mx.nd.topk(a, k=k))
   ...:

[  nan   nan   nan ... 2497. 2498. 2499.]
<NDArray 2504 @cpu(0)>

[0. 1. 2.]
<NDArray 3 @cpu(0)>

[0. 1. 2. 3.]
<NDArray 4 @cpu(0)>

[0.000e+00 1.000e+00 2.000e+00 3.000e+00 2.503e+03]
<NDArray 5 @cpu(0)>

[0.000e+00 1.000e+00 2.000e+00 3.000e+00 2.503e+03 2.502e+03]
<NDArray 6 @cpu(0)>

[0.000e+00 1.000e+00 2.000e+00 3.000e+00 2.503e+03 2.502e+03 2.501e+03]
<NDArray 7 @cpu(0)>

[0.000e+00 1.000e+00 2.000e+00 3.000e+00 2.503e+03 2.502e+03 2.501e+03
 2.500e+03]
<NDArray 8 @cpu(0)>

[0.000e+00 1.000e+00 2.000e+00 3.000e+00 2.503e+03 2.502e+03 2.501e+03
 2.500e+03 2.499e+03]
<NDArray 9 @cpu(0)>
```

Notice how the result is correct with mxnet version 1.2 but wrong on the master version. As a sidenote, even with mxnet version 1.2 the behavior of nd.topk is inconsistent with nd.max. The latter ignores 'nan' whereas the former considers it to be the maximum element.

```
n [9]: mx.nd.argmax(mx.nd.array([np.nan, 1]), axis=0)
Out[9]:

[1.]
<NDArray 1 @cpu(0)>

In [10]: mx.nd.topk(mx.nd.array([np.nan, 1]), axis=0)
Out[10]:

[0.]
<NDArray 1 @cpu(0)>


``` 

Arguably there was never a guarantee that nd.topk works under the presence of nan values, but legacy code may rely on this behavior and can break in unexpected ways.

@asmushetzel

issue_title: No support for slicing arrays along multiple axes
issue_body:
There is no support for slicing arrays along multiple axes among mxnet symbolic operators.
Currently it supports 

- https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.slice
- https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.slice_axis

The second operator accepts an int as axis parameter but not a tuple, so if we wanted to slice it along multiple axes, there is no operator to do it like here -
https://github.com/anirudhacharya/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Slice

issue_title: SymbolBlock has no `_reg_params` which cause save/load params fails
issue_body:
## Description
block with symbol block as child doesn't save/load symbol block's parameters.

## Environment info (Required)

mxnet==1.2.0b20180425

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon as gl, nd
from mxnet.gluon import nn


class Net(gl.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Net, self).__init__()
        with self.name_scope():
            backbone = gl.model_zoo.vision.resnet50_v1()
            data = mx.sym.var('data')
            featnames = ['stage1_activation2', 'stage2_activation3', 'stage3_activation5']
            out_names = ['_'.join([backbone.name, featname, 'output']) for featname in featnames]
            internals = backbone(data).get_internals()
            outs = [internals[out_name] for out_name in out_names]
            self.backbone = gl.SymbolBlock(outs, data, params=backbone.collect_params())
            self.body = nn.Conv2D(3, 1)
            
    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        x = self.body(x)
        return self.backbone(x)
    
ctx = mx.cpu()
net = Net()
net.initialize(mx.init.Normal(), ctx=ctx)
net.hybridize()

net(nd.random.normal(shape=(1, 3, 224, 224)))
net.save_params('./test.params')
for k, v in nd.load('./test.params').items():
    print(k)

for k, v in net.collect_params().items():
    print(k)
```

## What have you tried to solve it?

add `_reg_params` to symbol block solves this problem.

```python
self._reg_params = self._params
```

issue_title: Modify Python Logging
issue_body:
TL;DR: At present, the way logging is done causes problems with newer versions of the Jupyter/IPython notebook, among other things.

When I installed MXNet on a new machine recently and ran some notebooks to test it out I was somewhat confused because none of the callbacks for reporting training speed/accuracy were printed as output. 

For example, the [tutorial notebook has messages printed like](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/blob/master/example/notebooks/tutorial.ipynb):

```
INFO:root:Start training with [gpu(0)]
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Batch [200]  Speed: 70941.64 samples/sec Train-accuracy=0.389050
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Batch [400]  Speed: 97857.94 samples/sec Train-accuracy=0.646450
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Batch [600]  Speed: 70507.97 samples/sec Train-accuracy=0.743333
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Resetting Data Iterator
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Train-accuracy=0.743333
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Time cost=1.069
INFO:root:Epoch[0] Validation-accuracy=0.950800
...
```

However, these weren't showing up for me (although I recalled seeing them on my other machine).

This behavior turns out to be due to changes made to Jupyter's logging, where messages to the root logger (which is what gets called when invoking `logging.info`, `logging.debug`, etc.) no longer print to `stdout`, or maybe `stderr` doesn't show up in the notebook anymore.
[See the relevant Python docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.debug)

This can be fixed reasonably quickly via adding the following code at the top of the notebook:

``` python
import logging
import sys
root_logger = logging.getLogger()
stdout_handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
root_logger.addHandler(stdout_handler)
root_logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
```

But it's somewhat inelegant, since the root logger may be used by other modules whereas we presumably just want the log output from MXNet and the callbacks we are using.
It also causes issues in the documentation, where the [generated documents refer to objects by the full Python path](http://mxnet.readthedocs.org/en/latest/packages/python/module.html#mxnet.module.base_module.BaseModule), e.g.,

```
class mxnet.module.base_module.BaseModule(logger=<module 'logging' from '/usr/lib/python2.7/logging/__init__.pyc'>)
```

I am not sure about what the ideal fix ought to be, but maybe it would be better to define a specific logger for MXNet rather than just using the methods of the `logging` module directly.
If you guys want, I can put together a pull request at some point next week when I'm less busy.

issue_title: APIs that might be a good idea to remove in 2.0
issue_body:
Now that we decided to follow semantic versioning for releases, it would be a good idea to coordinate features and API changes to make the best use of the next major release. Thus, I propose that we use this issue to track the APIs we'd like to change in the next major version.

The candidates I've collected so far:
1. remove legacy ops such as batch-norm v1
2. reorganizing namespace for utility functions such as `download` in #9671 

Once there are more of such requests, I will try to organize these API-breaking requests better.

issue_title: Static URLs should be wrapped in static() so they resolve correctly in production environments
issue_body:
For my production environment, I store my static files on S3 using the following in settings:

STATICFILES_STORAGE = 'storages.backends.s3boto.S3BotoStorage'
DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE = 'storages.backends.s3boto.S3BotoStorage'
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = 'XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX'
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = 'YYYYYYYYYYYYYYYY'
AWS_QUERYSTRING_AUTH = False
AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = 'myproject-static'

For this to work correctly, I had to add the following import:

`from django.contrib.admin.templatetags.admin_static import static`

And I needed to wrap each of the media files in a static() call like the following:

```
def _media(self):
    js_files = [static('django_extensions/js/jquery.bgiframe.js'),
                static('django_extensions/js/jquery.ajaxQueue.js'),
                static('django_extensions/js/jquery.autocomplete.js')]

    return forms.Media(css={'all': (static('django_extensions/css/jquery.autocomplete.css'),)},
                           js=js_files)
```
The code in question is here:

https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/b6951d39736fc1d13e667c3a11f52f5c1764767d/django_extensions/admin/widgets.py#L27

issue_title: send_multipart mixes messages if some part of the message fails
issue_body:
If some part of the message doesn't provide a buffer interface, the send method will raise a TypeError exception. The problem is that may be some parts of the message that were already sent and the last part of the message without the SNDMORE flag will never be sent until the next message. 

E.g.:

Publisher
```python
publisher = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
publisher.bind('tcp://127.0.0.1:1234')
publisher.send_multipart(['a', 'b', object()])
>>> TypeError: <object object at 0x7f40bfe2a470> does not provide a buffer interface.
publisher.send_multipart(['c', 'd'])
```

Subscriber
```python
subscriber = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
subscriber.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, '')
subscriber.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:1234')
subscriber.recv_multipart()
>>> ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
```


I don't know which is the best solution but I wanted you to know that this is happening. 
From my app side, I'll be checking if the messages are 'bufferable' or not.

issue_title: sops sometimes fails to handle files with context
issue_body:
We've been seeing odd/intermittent issues with files that have context, so I generated some small test case files that have `working: ok` as the only thing in them.

We used the same KMS key (and only that KMS key) for all tests and I used sops 1.16 for the 1.x tests.

My test should result in a `-` for a success and `X` for a failure.

```
$ count=0; while true; do count=$((count+1)); if sops -d with_context.yml 1>/dev/null 2>/dev/null; then echo -n "-"; else echo -n "X"; fi; sleep 1; done
----X-X---X--X----X---X--XX--X-XXXX----X--
```

```
$ count=0; while true; do count=$((count+1)); if sops -d no_context.yml 1>/dev/null 2>/dev/null; then echo -n "-"; else echo -n "X"; fi; sleep 1; done
----------------------------
```

sops 3.0.2 with a sops 1.16-encrypted file (with context)
```
$ count=0; while true; do count=$((count+1)); if ./venv/bin/sops -d with_context_v1.yml 1>/dev/null 2>/dev/null; then echo -n "-"; else echo -n "X"; fi; sleep 1; done
--------------------------------
```

sops 1.16 file with sops v1 test:
```
$ count=0; while true; do count=$((count+1)); if ./venv/bin/sops -d with_context_v1.yml 1>/dev/null 2>/dev/null; then echo -n "-"; else echo -n "X"; fi; sleep 1; done
------------------------------
```

When running `sops -d with_context.yml`:
Success:
```
$ sops -d with_context.yml 
[AWSKMS]	 INFO[0000] Decryption succeeded                          arn="arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X"
[SOPS]	 INFO[0000] Data key recovered successfully              
working: ok
```
Failure:
```
$ sops -d with_context.yml 
[AWSKMS]	 WARN[0000] Decryption failed                             arn="arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X"
Failed to get the data key required to decrypt the SOPS file.

Group 0: FAILED
  arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X: FAILED
    - | Error decrypting key: InvalidCiphertextException: 
      | 	status code: 400, request id:
      | X
```

We log all error messages and when looking up that for the encryptionContext, somehow the `app` and `env` end up being the same value (the local sops yaml file shows two different values).

It feels like something isn't iterating over all possible encryption context keys.

Let me know if there's anything I can do to help/research.

For reference:

with_context.yml
```
working: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,tag:X,type:str]
sops:
    kms:
    -   arn: arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X
        context:
            app: X
            env: X
        created_at: '2018-04-03T01:19:34Z'
        enc: X
    gcp_kms: []
    lastmodified: '2018-04-03T01:35:47Z'
    mac: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,iv:X,tag:X,type:str]
    pgp: []
    unencrypted_suffix: _unencrypted
    version: 3.0.2
```

no_context.yml
```
working: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,tag:X,type:str]
sops:
    kms:
    -   arn: arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X
        created_at: '2018-04-03T01:28:38Z'
        enc: X
    gcp_kms: []
    lastmodified: '2018-04-03T01:28:46Z'
    mac: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,iv:X,tag:X,type:str]
    pgp: []
    unencrypted_suffix: _unencrypted
    version: 3.0.2
```

with_context_v1.yml
```
working: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,iv:X,tag:X,type:str]
sops:
    attention: This section contains key material that should only be modified with
        extra care. See `sops -h`.
    version: '1.16'
    unencrypted_suffix: _unencrypted
    kms:
    -   arn: arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:X:key/X
        context:
            app: X
            env: X
        enc: X
        created_at: '2018-04-03T01:39:57Z'
    lastmodified: '2018-04-03T01:39:57Z'
    mac: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:X,iv:X,tag:X,type:str]
```

issue_title: Implement functional tests
issue_body:
We currently have unit tests (albeit not so many) for all modules in sops, but there's nothing testing `cmd/sops`. Ideally, we should have some:

### Basic tests

- [ ] Test encrypting a file, that it's actually encrypted and all keys are preserved.
- [ ] Test decrypting files from all previous and current versions of sops.
- [ ] Test encrypting and then decrypting a file.
- [ ] Test rotating a file.
- [ ] Test editing a file (will need a fake editor, maybe just `sed`).

### Regression tests

We've encountered several issues with main.go so far. We should have included tests for these when we fixed them, but there wasn't any kind of test framework set up for it.

- [ ] https://github.com/mozilla/sops/issues/155

https://github.com/mozilla/bleach/blob/19dd5ba885e5429cef63a4531675a73ca668edcc/bleach/sanitizer.py#L186-L203

This can happen when running migration in Django (1.11) in Python 2.7 using the [html_sanitizer](https://github.com/ui/django-html_sanitizer) app. The model field in question is [SanitizedTextField](https://github.com/ui/django-html_sanitizer/blob/master/sanitizer/models.py#L32) although I doubt this is of importance here.

Not sure if this is the desired behavior, if not the `if not text:` clause from L201 could simply be moved above the instance check.

issue_title: Continue checkpointed training at correct next sample index
issue_body:
After resuming a checkpoint, training batches/samples start over at index 0 and by that curriculum learning notion is violated.
One has to calculate the correct sample index in `start_coordination()` based on the the current training `step` and derived values.

issue_body:

issue_title: Raise severities for cloudwatch alarms
issue_body:
Cloudwatch has only two severities: `OK` and `ALARM` plu one informative `INSUFFICIENT_DATA`. Since `ALARM` is the highest severity I would raise it to `critical`, `INSUFFICIENT_DATA` to `major` and OK leave as `normal`. 

The rationale by this is that  since ALARM is used for alarms, it should have the highest priority. 

This can be done either statically, just as now or by supplying severty map in config. I can as always provide a PR for both.

issue_title: Provisional Warnings - Addressing the Pace of Developement
issue_body:
With the pace of `traitlets` development in recent months, there's been a lot of concern over ease of adoption, and the ability of those who already have, to keep up with the each new release. To reduce this unease, It's been suggested that we implement "provisional warnings" which will raise an exception explaining the instability of various features by default, but that can be manually silenced with a context manager. 

The purpose of these warnings would be to create a space where `traitlets` can develop at a natural rate (fast or slow), but do so behind bold warning tape. A provisional warning will explain to any developer so bold as to bypass it, that the feature they are accessing is not guaranteed to exist over any period of time in the future, whether that be in the same form, or at all.

I hope to determine:
1. Whether provisional warnings are reasonable
2. What features ought to have provisional warnings
3. How provisional warnings should be implemented.

issue_title: Writing ~/.pydistutils.cfg affects parallel builds.
issue_body:
In build systems that allow products to build multiple Python packages (apps) together in parallel, such as PyGradle, the solution from #16 breaks the build of a package that does not use shiv. It was implemented in #27 but we did not notice until now the negative effect it has.

Since that solution is implemented only for homebrew Python, I believe it's unacceptable that it breaks standard Python builds and packages. We need to make this usage conditional, with some setting, so that users who need it can apply it, but the standard usage should not be doing this by default.

I'm inclined to just plain revert #27 to get the fix out ASAP.
Users who need this change can use the releases of shiv that have it, and later can use the future release of shiv that implements this conditionally.

Can we execute this plan promptly?
Thanks!

issue_title: String with encoding
issue_body:
There seems to be some inconsistencies with respect to the `encoding` argument on `String` objects.  For example (in py3):
```
>>> import colander
>>> s = colander.String('utf-8')
>>> s.serialize(None, 123)
'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, '123')
b'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, str(123))
b'123'
```
in py2:
```
>>> import colander
>>> s = colander.String('utf-8')
>>> s.serialize(None, 123)
u'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, '123')
'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, unicode(123))
'123'
```
Basically, if you pass something that isn't an instance of one of the python string types you can bypass the encoding step.

The docs say:
> A non-Unicode input value to ``serialize`` is converted to a
> Unicode using the encoding (``unicode(value, encoding)``);
> subsequently the Unicode object is reeencoded to a ``str``
> object using the encoding and returned.

This seems to be a regression as it appears it did work properly in previous versions.

`SELECT `source` AS `source`, `target` AS `target`, SUM(value) AS `mysum`

issue_title: Cleaning up locating windows
issue_body:
How to locate windows is pretty complicated and not very well documented. There are also lot of undocumented and untested features. This issue covers cleaning up the functionality and, very importantly, documenting it adequately.

As part of the cleanup, undocumented features that are not considered useful are removed. This is obviously backwards incompatible, but because these are undocumented features, it's very unlikely that they are used widely. This is the functionality that is going to be removed:

- Using string `null` as a locator. Use Python `None` instead or, much better, omit argument altogether.
- Matching window names, titles and URLs case-insensitively.
-

issue_title: Add support for pip's --target
issue_body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Python AWS lambda packages require their dependencies to be packaged with the source, which is suggested to be done using `pip install -t <package` (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-python-how-to-create-deployment-package.html). This means you can't use pipenv directly to manage their dependencies, because it doesn't support the `-t/--target` option.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
`pipenv install -t path/to/src <package>` installs a dependency into `Pipfile/Pipfile.lock`, and runs `pip install -t path/to/src <package>`.

I think it'd make sense not to do the equivalent of `pipenv install <package>` at the same time, ie it'd be like only running `pip -t` and match its behaviour. Just the dependency would end up managed by pipenv.

Note that pip does't have a `pip uninstall -t`, I think it'd be okay not to have a `pipenv uninstall -t` also.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
My current workaround is to use `pipenv lock -r > requirements.txt` and then `pip install -r requirements.txt -t path/to/src`.

**Additional context**
I assume this would depend on a fix to https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/2444, because on MacOS `pip install -t <package>` requires a setup.cfg to be added, so `pipenv -t` would be the same - see that AWS link for details on that.

issue_title: Automatically download and cache new pip/setuptools versions
issue_body:
With the faster release cycle pip and (to some extent) setuptools nowadays enjoy it's become increasingly likely that newly created virtualenvs will contain an outdated pip version. 

I would suggest that virtualenv should stop bundling a specific pip/setuptools version and instead download and cache the respective current version. 

Later if later no internet connectivity is available, the cached version can be used, otherwise a version check can be performed and the user be prompted to update.

Producer finaliser may fail to flush queue, hitting ReferenceError
issue_body:
I just ran into a bug on the rdkafka branch, where queue-flushing fails due to the use of weakref (as @emmett9001 had warned about). We don't have test coverage for it, and I only noticed it by coincidence, spotting an unimportant-looking debugging log message.

In theory at least, the same bug should exist in `pykafka.Producer`, as of merging #329. This is not a regression, because before #329 we would never run the finaliser, so we would also never wait to flush the queue at program exit, but it needs fixing of course.

The `SimpleConsumer` and `BalancedConsumer` (which were also patched in #329) do not suffer from this problem: the only state they carry that needs flushing are the offsets, and we explicitly commit offsets on the thread that calls `stop()`, so we really don't care for weakref exceptions on the worker threads.

issue_title: Help reproducing fitparams task
issue_body:
Disclaimer: _This is not an issue with `photutils`. I'm just out of ideas and I don't where else to turn for help. I will therefore understand completely if this is closed on the spot. That said, I will really appreciate any help you could give me, since I've been trying to figure this out for weeks now._

--------------------------------------------------------------------------
I'm trying to reproduce the [fitparams](http://stsdas.stsci.edu/cgi-bin/gethelp.cgi?fitparams) task from the `photcal` package.

I've already sent en email to the STScI (the developers of Pyraf), posted in the [IRAF forum](http://iraf.net/forum/viewtopic.php?msg=1&showtopic=1469632), and even wrote to Lindsey Davis. The STScI guys told me they don't work with this package and I should ask in the forum, the forum post has been sitting there for ~20 days (it doesn't look like it's going to receive an answer), and I never got a response from Dr Davis.

In page 32 of the [A user's guide to stellar CCD photometry with IRAF](https://www.mn.uio.no/astro/english/services/it/help/visualization/iraf/daophot2.pdf)  guide, an output of doing a `:vshow` in `fitparams` is shown in Fig 27:

![captura realizada el 2017-05-23 16 36 17](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6054951/26372927/fd75dfe0-3fd5-11e7-9cda-03c915157da2.png)

Where `function, fit` are the observed and predicted values in the best found least squares regression, `residuals=function-fit`, and I have no idea how `sigma` is obtained or what it represents (issue 1. below). I'd like to reproduce the statistical values found for the fit. I can (almost) reproduce most of these:

```
standard deviation 0.0214969
reduced chi        1.0036573
average error      0.0214577
average scatter    ?
RMS                0.0183326
```


 as follows :

* **standard deviation**

      sum_sq = np.sum((function - fit) ** 2)
      nu = len(function) - deg
      stddev = np.sqrt(sum_sq / nu)

   where `deg=3` since 3 parameters parameters are being fitted.

* **reduced chi**

      chisq = np.sum(((function - fit) / sigma) ** 2)
      nu = len(function) - deg
      red_chi = chisq / nu

* **average error**

      avrg_error = stddev / np.sqrt(red_chi)


* **average scatter**

      avrg_scatter = ?

* **RMS**

      RMS = np.sqrt(((function - fit) ** 2).mean())

The issues I find are:


1. I don't know how the `sigma` column (rightmost column in Fig 27) is obtained. Following the trace, I see that the `iraf/pkg/xtools/inlfit/ingresults.gx` file writes to that column the following values:

        (sqrt (PIXEL (1.0) / Mem$t[wts1+i-1]))

   I assume `Mem$t[wts1+i-1]` is some kind of standard deviation? I tried diving in deeper into the code, but the language used (some kind of mixture between `C` and `Fortran`?) looks like an impossible mess to decipher to me.
1. I can not reproduce the `average scatter` value. I have no idea what formula is used. I've tried several to no avail.
1. Most of the statistical parameters shown above (except the `average scatter`) are *almost* reproduced using those equations. There are visible differences nonetheless,  which I can only assign to floating point rounding errors by `fitparams` (as long as the formulas shown above are correct).

issue_title: Suggestion on the improvements on centroiding functions
issue_body:
Currently, the centroiding functions, which are designed to find the centroid of a single source image, calculates the centroid by using input ``data`` and ``mask``. 
It does suffice all the needs, but it is possible to improve it slightly and gain much convenience. **In short, I think it will be helpful if we put some "semi-automatic" masking functionality into the centroiding functions.**

This contains motivation, simple solution, demonstrations (of how the "solution" works), discussion, and conclusion. I want to ask if this makes sense, and if so, I want to start implement this functionality and make a pull request.

# Motivation
In the image below, there are pixels even below 0, so it is desirable to mask such pixels properly. But even if we mask all those pixels (to value 0), there are some noisy pixels which have positive value, and thus the centroid will be slightly shifted towards bottom left direction. Therefore, we need certain threshold to mask "background" or "noisy" pixels, so that we are taking only "source-dominated" pixels when calculating the centroid. 

![figure_1-1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17214293/31317533-8d639d7c-ac7d-11e7-8551-4fce235fa19b.png)

Currently, it must be done by the user and put it as the ``mask``. But many users may forget to take care of ``mask`` or how to choose proper threshold for ``mask``.

# Simple Solution
Reference [1] has shown that the best way to find the centroid is to set the threshold as ``noise_mean + 3 * noise_std``. **Thus, I propose centroiding functions to have an option to take this semi-automatic masking functionality.**

For test, I used only ``centroid_com``. I put three new parameters to it, namely, ``noise_mean``, ``noise_std``, and ``thresh_sigma``, and named this test function as ``centroid_com2`` for this moment:
```python
def centroid_com2(data, mask=None, noise_mean=0., noise_std=0, 
                 thresh_sigma=3.):
    """
    Calculate the centroid of a 2D array as its "center of mass"
    determined from image moments.

    Invalid values (e.g. NaNs or infs) in the ``data`` array are
    automatically masked.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data : array_like
        The 2D array of the image.

    mask : array_like (bool), optional
        A boolean mask, with the same shape as ``data``, where a `True`
        value indicates the corresponding element of ``data`` is masked.

    noise_mean : float, optional
        The averaged noise level. The simplest way to calculate it is to take
        the median or mean of the ``data`` after 3-sigma clipping with few 
        iterations. This, however, may overestimate the noise level (since the
        nearby pixels are brightened by the light source), so it is best to use
        the pixels well outside the source to estimate background level. 
        Defaults to 0.
    
    noise_std : float, optional
        The fluctuation (standard deviation) of noise (error source). It can
        also be calculated as ``noise_mean`` by using sigma clipping, but it
        is better to use pixels well apart from the source. Defaults to 0.
    
    thresh_sigma : float, optional
        The threshold above the ``noise`` to mask pixels for centroiding. Only 
        the pixels having value above ``noise_mean + thresh_sigma * noise_std`` 
        will be used for the centroiding. It is known that the 3-sigma
        criterion, i.e., ``noise + 3 * noise_sigma``, is a good threshold 
        (Ma et al. 2009, Optics Express, 17, 8525), so the recommended value is 
        ``thresh_sigma=3``. Defaults to 3.

    Returns
    -------
    centroid : `~numpy.ndarray`
        The ``x, y`` coordinates of the centroid.
    """

    from skimage.measure import moments
    
    data = np.ma.asanyarray(data)

    if mask is not None and mask is not np.ma.nomask:
        mask = np.asanyarray(mask)
        if data.shape != mask.shape:
            raise ValueError('data and mask must have the same shape.')
        data.mask |= mask
    
    mask_noise = data < (noise_mean + thresh_sigma * noise_std)
    data.mask |= mask_noise
    print('threshold =', noise_mean + thresh_sigma * noise_std)
    print('N_masked =', np.count_nonzero(data.mask))
    
    if np.any(~np.isfinite(data)):
        data = np.ma.masked_invalid(data)
        warnings.warn('Input data contains input values (e.g. NaNs or infs), '
                      'which were automatically masked.', AstropyUserWarning)

    # Convert the data to a float64 (double) `numpy.ndarray`,
    # which is required for input to `skimage.measure.moments`.
    # Masked values are set to zero.
    data = data.astype(np.float)
    data.fill_value = 0.
    data = data.filled()

    m = moments(data, 1)
    xcen = m[1, 0] / m[0, 0]
    ycen = m[0, 1] / m[0, 0]
    
    return np.array([xcen, ycen])
```
I intentionally put some print functions for test. The only differences from the original version are the docstring and the four lines containing ``mask_noise`` and print functions.

# Demonstration
I want to show some examples to convince the importance of masking. 

## Demo 1
For the first demonstration, I want to use the data used by the [documentation](https://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/stable/photutils/centroids.html).

In the following, ``x1, y1``, ``x2, y2``, and ``x3, y3`` are the same as the documentation (centroid com, 1dg, and 2dg). 
``x4, y4`` is the position of centroid when the pixel mask is done for ``value < 0``. 
``x5, y5`` is when the pixels are masked based on the Reference's criterion with true noise mean and std (5, 5, respectively; see [here](https://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/photutils.datasets.make_4gaussians_image.html#photutils.datasets.make_4gaussians_image)), so this can be regarded as the true centroid measured by com. 
Finally, ``x6, y6`` is when the masking is based on inference on noise mean and std (3-sigma clipping using ``astropy.stats.sigma_clipped_stats``).

The locations from ``centroid_com2`` are shown by ``x`` marker.

```python
from photutils.datasets import make_4gaussians_image
from photutils import centroid_com, centroid_1dg, centroid_2dg
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from astropy.stats import sigma_clipped_stats

data = make_4gaussians_image()[43:79, 76:104]    # extract single object
xy_str = '{:s}: ({:.5f}, {:.5f})'
x1, y1 = centroid_com(data)
print(xy_str.format('centroid_com', x1, y1))
x2, y2 = centroid_1dg(data)
print(xy_str.format('centroid_1dg', x2, y2))
x3, y3 = centroid_2dg(data)
print(xy_str.format('centroid_2dg', x3, y3))
print('-'*80)
# default values:
x4, y4 = centroid_com2(data, noise_mean=0, noise_std=0) 
print(xy_str.format('centroid_com2', x4, y4))
print('-'*80)
# if we know the true values:
print('True noise (mean, med, std) = 5.0, 5.0, 5.0')
x5, y5 = centroid_com2(data, noise_mean=5, noise_std=5, thresh_sigma=3) 
print(xy_str.format('centroid_com2', x5, y5))
print('-'*80)
# if we do not know the true values:
noise_mean, noise_med, noise_std = sigma_clipped_stats(make_4gaussians_image(), 
                                                       sigma=3, iters=50)
print('Estimated noise (mean, med, std) =', 
      noise_mean, noise_med, noise_std)
x6, y6 = centroid_com2(data, noise_mean=noise_mean, noise_std=noise_std, 
                       thresh_sigma=3) 
print(xy_str.format('centroid_com2', x6, y6))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1)
ax.imshow(data, origin='lower', interpolation='nearest', cmap='viridis')
marker = '+'
ms, mew = 30, 2.
plt.plot(x1, y1, color='#1f77b4', marker=marker, ms=ms, mew=mew)
plt.plot(x2, y2, color='#17becf', marker=marker, ms=ms, mew=mew)
plt.plot(x3, y3, color='#d62728', marker=marker, ms=ms, mew=mew)
plt.plot(x4, y4, color='r', marker='x', ms=20, mew=mew)
plt.plot(x5, y5, color='k', marker='x', ms=20, mew=mew)
plt.plot(x6, y6, color='b', marker='x', ms=20, mew=mew)
```
I skipped the zoomed-in image part. The result is as follows:
```
centroid_com: (13.93158, 17.05123)
centroid_1dg: (14.04035, 16.96231)
centroid_2dg: (14.00221, 16.99613)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
threshold = 0.0
N_masked = 19
centroid_com2: (13.93009, 17.05271)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
True noise (mean, med, std) = 5.0, 5.0, 5.0
threshold = 20
N_masked = 366
centroid_com2: (13.99611, 17.00239)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Estimated noise (mean, med, std) = 5.49662890322 5.40802826546 5.2490393209
threshold = 21.2437468659
N_masked = 392
centroid_com2: (13.99089, 16.98284)
```
![figure_1-2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17214293/31317684-5f8b438e-ac80-11e7-988c-06a68cdc707a.png)

As can be seen, all are consistent with each other. (confirmed ``centroid_com2`` works well)

* accepted: all

## Demo 2
For the second demonstration, I used the first image (source at the corner of the data rather than center), i.e., ``data = make_4gaussians_image()[10:30, 130:160]`` (all other code lines identical). Then
```
centroid_com: (18.49531, 13.30201)
centroid_1dg: (19.99518, 15.00513)
centroid_2dg: (20.01413, 15.00670)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
threshold = 0.0
N_masked = 75
centroid_com2: (18.42292, 13.19838)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
True noise (mean, med, std) = 5.0, 5.0, 5.0
threshold = 20
N_masked = 481
centroid_com2: (20.02485, 14.74312)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Estimated noise (mean, med, std) = 5.49662890322 5.40802826546 5.2490393209
threshold = 21.2437468659
N_masked = 488
centroid_com2: (19.99919, 14.80074)
```
![figure_1-3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17214293/31317729-1f73cc3e-ac81-11e7-8c6e-7e273ecec031.png)

Now it is visible that the original ``centroid_com`` (skyblue plus) and the default ``centroid_com2``(red cross) are not satisfactory. All other (including original 1dg, 2dg) coincide with each other.

* accepted: 1dg, 2dg, and ``centroid_com2`` with proper input

## Demo 3
Now I use the star image:
```
from photutils.datasets import load_star_image
data = load_star_image()[520:540, 385:400]
```
and all other code lines identical, but I erased ``x5, y5`` since we don't have true noise value. Then 
```
centroid_com: (7.38774, 10.35603)
centroid_1dg: (-11.42871, 15.42895)
centroid_2dg: (8.39294, 15.40005)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
threshold = 0.0
N_masked = 0
centroid_com2: (7.38774, 10.35603)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Estimated noise (mean, med, std) = 3768.70797549 3744.0 234.867366443
threshold = 4473.31007482
N_masked = 185
centroid_com2: (9.12297, 13.18774)
```
![figure_1-5](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17214293/31318340-90e4044c-ac8b-11e7-811e-c881427562b1.png)

The ``centroid_2dg`` seems to be fallen into a strange local minimum, which is unexpected. 
The ``centroid_1dg`` gives the most reliable value, while ``centroid_com2`` with proper input (blue cross) is also marginally acceptable.

* accepted: 1dg
* ``centroid_com2`` with proper intput can marginally be accepted.

## Demo 4
I now want to use an observed data of a galaxy UGC 1324 (observed by KMTNet/CTIO, observed on UT 2015-12-21). The result is
```
centroid_com: (149.05460, 101.70518)
centroid_1dg: (79.61474, 115.93930)
centroid_2dg: (144.21234, 116.90994)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
threshold = 0.0
N_masked = 0
centroid_com2: (149.05460, 101.70518)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Estimated noise (mean, med, std) = 429.618985696 428.903198242 17.935913277
threshold = 483.426725527
N_masked = 39002
centroid_com2: (147.93215, 113.94106)
```
![figure_1-6](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17214293/31318603-fb34b270-ac8f-11e7-918d-661457ca0bcb.png)
``centroid_1dg`` now gave the worst result (the left cyan plus marker). Maybe because of a strange local minimum.
Now ``centroid_2dg`` gives the best result: the center of bulge.
``centroid_com`` is at the edge of bulge, which is not good. Same goes true for the ``centroid_com2`` with default parameters.
``centroid_com2`` with proper input also gave reasonable result. 

* accepted: ``centroid_2dg``, ``centroid_com2`` with proper input.

# Discussion
From the demonstrations (and I tried hundreds of different cases by myself) the ``centroid_1dg`` seems the best centroid-finding function, and the only exception was the last demonstration. Unfortunately, Gaussian fits always have possibility of getting stuck in unexpected local minima, so we need care. 

The ``centroid_com2``, which uses automatic masking by threshold given by the Ref[1], is very reliable since there is no possibility for it to fall into a local minimum. It usually gives good guess on centroids for all possible cases, unless strong secondary source is in the data. 

Possible way to get around the local minima issue of Gaussian fit is to accept centroid results only when it's in the image, and make the data size to be very small. This is what many astronomers do for non-extended sources, such as [IRAF](http://stsdas.stsci.edu/cgi-bin/gethelp.cgi?centerpars):
> cbox = 5.(scale units)
>   The width of the subraster used for object centering in units of the DATAPARS scale parameter. Cbox must be big enough to include a reasonable number of pixels for center determination but not so large so as to include a lot of noise. Recommended initial values are 2.5-4.0 * the FWHM of the PSF value. 
This ``cbox`` is related to the size of the input ``data`` for photutils' centroiding functions. 

# Conclusion
The importance of masking is clear from the demonstrations (by seeing how the ``centroid_com`` results improved when updated to ``centroid_com2``). 
Thus it will be helpful if making ``mask`` becomes easier. I thus suggest to put some functionality to centroiding functions.

Any opinions are welcomed :)

[1] Ma et al. 2009, "Error analysis of CCD-based point source centroid computation under the background light", Optics Express, 17, 8525

issue_title: package_data dict passed to `setup()` in nupic's setup.py may be superfluous
issue_body: The  `package_data` dict passed to `setup()` in nupic's setup.py may be superfluous. In PR #2617, my code is loading a .json file from "nupic/frameworks/opf/common_models/anomaly_params_random_encoder". This json file is covered by the rule `recursive-include src/nupic *.capnp *.json *.tpl *.txt *.xml` in nupic's MANIFEST.in, but not covered by any rule in `package_data`. Despite this omission from `package_data`, all tests passed, and I verified that the tar.gz file generatred by a local `setup.py sdist` build includes that .json file.

issue_title: SP Perf: Stop making big lists of neighbors for every column
issue_body:
Local inhibition is currently very slow. For every column, we create a list of all columns in its neighborhood. The vast majority of the SP's time is spent creating this list.

This list doesn't need to exist. We should just iterate through the neighborhood without ever putting it in a list.

issue_title: SparseMatrix should allow nNonZerosPerRow with a list of rows
issue_body:
Currently `nNonZerosPerRow` returns the number of nonzeros for every row. There should be another similar API that takes a list of rows and gets their nonzero counts. My current plan is that this will be a new overload of the `nNonZerosPerRow` method.

This enables us to check how many synapses are on a set of segments / cells. We can use this to implement a `maxSynapsesPerSegment` in Python.

issue_title: No validation error is raised on PATCH with unknown attribute
issue_body:
With PUT, Cornice is in charge of validating against view schema. Works fine:

```
echo '{"title": "change"}' | http PUT "http://0.0.0.0:8888/v1/buckets/default/collections/articles/records/ff7db2ad-cc85-4715-b563-1579d4c27c3b" --auth=user:pass
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Backoff, Retry-After, Alert
Content-Length: 231
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8
Date: Fri, 10 Jul 2015 10:45:42 GMT
Server: waitress

{
    "code": 400, 
    "details": [
        {
            "description": "data is missing", 
            "location": "body", 
            "name": "data"
        }, 
        {
            "description": "title is not allowed", 
            "location": "body", 
            "name": "title"
        }
    ], 
    "errno": 107, 
    "error": "Invalid parameters", 
    "message": "data is missing"
}
```

With PATCH, it does not raise:
```
echo '{"title": "change"}' | http PATCH "http://0.0.0.0:8888/v1/buckets/default/collections/articles/records/ff7db2ad-cc85-4715-b563-1579d4c27c3b" --auth=user:pass
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Backoff, Retry-After, Alert
Content-Length: 202
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8
Date: Fri, 10 Jul 2015 10:47:45 GMT
Etag: "1436525081662"
Last-Modified: Fri, 10 Jul 2015 10:44:41 GMT
Server: waitress

{
    "data": {
        "id": "ff7db2ad-cc85-4715-b563-1579d4c27c3b", 
        "last_modified": 1436525081662, 
        "title": "toto"
    }, 
    "permissions": {
        "write": [
            "basicauth:631c2d625ee5726172cf67c6750de10a3e1a04bcd603bc9ad6d6b196fa8257a6"
        ]
    }
}
```

**Explanation**:

Since the PATCH request payload does not always match schema, Cliquet does define any schema for the PATCH endpoint.
Instead, it validates the result of merge (old record + changes) against the resource mapping (*which BTW duplicates some Cornice code to add colander errors to the response*).

But the resource mapping is not the same as the schema that was used by the ``ViewSet``. This should be fixed.

issue_title: moving to enum.Enum
issue_body:
We have an ad-hoc Enum class at https://github.com/mozilla-services/cliquet/blob/master/cliquet/utils.py#L152

it behaves very differently from Python 3.4 Enum - https://docs.python.org/3/library/enum.html and even makes our Project shadow the stdlib Enum in 3.4

I don't see any good reason to keep ours, as we can use "enum34" which backports enum in all Python version.

However, that's not a small change - so I'd like to ask here before doing it - in case I am missing a good reason to keep it

issue_title: WebSearch: find eprint astro-ph/0101431 or nucl-th/0204034
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-03-14

The precedence rules for regexp queries vs Boolean queries are currently such as user searches for:

```
find eprint astro-ph/0101431 or nucl-th/0204034
```

on the demo site breaks.  

This is because slashes look like regexp queries, while most users would expect `OR` to take precedence here.

However, see also queries like:

```
title:/foo or bar/
```

when defining what a good default behaviour should look like.

In order to quick fix this situation, we may want to rely on the fact than arXiv numbers have alphanumeric characters just before and just after slash, while in the other cases
there is a white space character to either side of the slash.

P.S. As usual, a test case for the above query (that matches Atlantis demo site records) should be written as part of this ticket.

issue_title: api: empty _files field added in deposit but not in record
issue_body:
**Problem:**
When publishing a deposit for the first time. If the deposit has no files, it will still get an `_files` field added automatically and this field won't be added in the published record.

**Why this happens:**
`Deposit._publish_new` calls `Deposit. _process_files` which calls `self.files` which calls `invenio_records_files. FilesIterator` which [modifies the deposit itself by adding an `_files` field](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/master/invenio_records_files/api.py#L108).
As [`Deposit._publish_new` had already made a copy of the deposit data](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/master/invenio_deposit/api.py#L278), this new field is not added to the published record.

The impact is low but this is still a discrepancy as the published record should be an exact copy of the deposit when published.

Now the question is:
- do we remove the creation of the `_files` field from `self.files`? This in my opinion is the best design as an iterator should not modify the collection it iterates over. (This is similar to invenio-records-files/issues#21 where a getter creates the bucket)
- or do we add by default an empty `_files` field to the published record `data`? Simpler solution but kind of ugly as one has to dig in invenio-records-files to understand why it is needed. This might create bugs later when code is refactored (as it always is one day or the other).

```
aracter maps to <undefined>
```

issue_title: Using zope.index to find similar documents efficiently
issue_body:
Hi! Thanks for this awesome code. I have a question about using zope.textindex to find similar documents to a target document. I want to find the indexed document that is most similar to a query document. 

I can do this by turning the document into a big OR string (see below). Is there a more efficient way to find the most similar documents when the tokens present in the query document are not a strict subset of the tokens in the target document?

```python
>>> from zope.index.text.textindex import TextIndex
>>> index = TextIndex()
>>> index.index_doc(1, "silver pearl splitter")
>>> index.apply("silver pearl splayer") # this doesn't work
BTrees.IFBTree.IFBucket([])
>>> index.apply(" OR ".join("silver pearl splayer").split()) # This does
```

issue_title: 4.4.1 Regression: `Components._utilities`/`_utility_registrations` can get out of sync with the cache
issue_body:
The `_UtilityRegistrations` object makes a reference to the `Component`'s `_utilities` and `_utility_registrations` when it is created. The `Component` object holds a strong reference to the `_UtilityRegistrations`. Presumably the `_UtilityRegistrations` holds these two references to avoid holding a reference to the `Component` instance and thus introducing a cycle.

The problem introduced in 4.4.1 is that if either of those two instance variables are assigned to, the cache object is not updated. Previously, accessing the cache would compare the identity of the objects it held with the identity of the objects the Component held and reset if they changed. Now that doesn't happen.

The use case is that subclasses may want to change the identity of `_utility_registrations` in the `registerUtility` method. Specifically, we have a subclass that will change the `_utility_registrations` object to a BTree instead of a dict when it grows to a certain size.

Now we *could* update our subclass to have a dependency on >= 4.4.1 and knowledge of the fact that the cache is now an instance attribute that also needs to be updated, but I'd prefer to see it handled here so that the cache is always consistent and guaranteed to be so.

issue_title: JavaMultipleMethod mismatch for Interface arguments
issue_body:
JavaMultipleMethod resolves to a wrong method signature in a case where matching should be done by the interfaces. Consider the following [Cipher.init](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/javax/crypto/Cipher.html):

        Cipher = autoclass("javax.crypto.Cipher")
        SecretKeySpec = autoclass("javax.crypto.spec.SecretKeySpec")

        default_key = [0x00] * 16
        secret_key_spec = SecretKeySpec(default_key, "DESede")

        # The signature we want is (int, java.security.Key) [Key is interface]
        # the signature this resolves is (int, java.security.Certificate)
        # JavaException: Invalid instance of u'javax/crypto/spec/SecretKeySpec' passed for a u'java/security/cert/Certificate'
        cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, secret_key_spec)
 
A (temporary) solution would be manually pull out the correct underlying call signature from `JavaMultipleMethod` object using the interface name in the signature. However is this possible? I did not find any examples doing this and my poking from console did not yield results.

issue_title: Old toolchain is now deprecated
issue_body:
Buildozer now uses the current p4a toolchain automatically, and I haven't even seen anyone discussing the old toolchain for some time. As such, the next release will officially fully deprecate the old toolchain.

I've opened this issue as a reminder, both for the issue notes, and because as part of this we should:

- Close all issues and PRs relating only to the old toolchain
- Make sure the documentation is updated if necessary

issue_title: Use find_packages() to automatically find packages
issue_body:
Hello,

in a recent issue I noticed some problem with package
https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/issues/97

I submited a PR https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/pull/98
which have been merged by @davidastephens 

I was replacing

    packages=['pandas_datareader'],

by

    packages=['pandas_datareader',
        'pandas_datareader.google', 'pandas_datareader.yahoo'
    ],

I wonder why not doing

     from setuptools import setup, find_packages

and

    packages=find_packages(exclude=['contrib', 'docs', 'tests*']),

So if we add other package we won't have to modify `setup.py`

Your opinion ?

Kind regards

issue_title: Multithreaded brotli within 7-Zip
issue_body:
I have just released a new version of [7-Zip ZS](https://github.com/mcmilk/7-Zip-zstd).

Within the 7z-Zip container, the threading is done with [skippable frames](https://github.com/mcmilk/zstdmt) like in [ZStandard](https://github.com/facebook/zstd/).

All levels (0..11) use a default windows size of 24.

Here is a screenshot of it:
![Methods](https://mcmilk.de/projects/7-Zip-zstd/Methods.png "Methods with Brotli")

Compression / decompression of `.br` files can be added also, when I have some MAGIC's therefore.

I have done also some benchmarking:
![Compression Speed vs Ratio](https://mcmilk.de/projects/7-Zip-zstd/dl/compr-v120.png "Compression Speed vs Ratio")
![Decompression Speed](https://mcmilk.de/projects/7-Zip-zstd/dl/decomp-v120.png "Decompression Speed per Level")

Does the implementation look okay for you, or are there any issues with it ?

While using it, I've found myself building a [simple wrapper class](https://gist.github.com/jackmaney/4a98cfdfef61e1d5a097) that geocodes a list of addresses and enforces a per-second rate limit (the actual version that I'm using has a few more bells and whistles, but this is the basic idea). The resulting object takes a `geocoder` object and uses the geopy api (namely calling a `geocode` method on the `geocoder` and passing in an address from the given list).

Would a pull request to add such a class be welcome?

issue_title: cutout_row/cutout_col for MEDS output type when using offsets.
issue_body:
I believe the cutout_row and cutout_col columns in the OBJECT_DATA extension of a MEDS file should give the (zero-offset) position of the object in pixel coordinates (@esheldon can confirm).  Currently this is set to the origin of the stamp wcs in WriteMeds in des_meds.py (called 'row0' and 'col0' in des_meds.py, see e.g. line 263). 

I think this behavior will become problematic as soon as you have more than one exposure of an object with different offsets. I think the solution is to have MEDSBuilder access the stamp offsets (I think this could be done with an extra output type, but maybe there is an easier way?), and make these an attribute of MultiExposureObject. Then WriteMEDS would also be able to access this info. I can go ahead and try to implement this, but keen to hear if @rmjarvis @esheldon or others have thoughts on this before I do so.

issue_title: Using results of Poisson CCD Simulator to simulate BF effect
issue_body: Craig Lage at UC Davis has developed a Poisson equation solver that computes the distortion of the pixel edges as the electrons get collected in the CCD pixels. In the DESC Hack day today, we made some progress towards integrating his code (in C++) with the C++ layer of the GalSim. We identified  that the natural way to include this functionality would be by tweaking the photon-shooting method inside the `drawImage` routine.  I will push the code after some cleanup, but this is not completely done yet.

issue_title: Mark run ids archived to Glacier as processed
issue_body:
One problem here is that it doesn't seems possible to check if some prefix like `enriched-archive/run=2017-04-27-14-39-42/` was archived to glacier by simply listing `enriched-archive` and checking metadata. It's possible to archive *object*, not *prefix*.

Therefore, we most likely need to:
1. List prefixes in enriched archive
2. For each of prefix get `part-00001` key
3. Check if this key has storage class `GLACIER`
4. If it does - mark as processed, if doesn't - unprocessed, if key doesn't exist - unprocessed (?)

Approach seems a bit fragile because for some keys in prefix can probably be archived and some can be not. Not sure how likely.

Another approach would be to get lifecycle policy for bucket. And based on prefix, current date, and date in run id decide if this prefix is archived. This approach is fragile because it's not possible restore and reprocess run id.

@alexanderdean few more questions:
1. Should we just not return archived run ids from `list_run_ids` or add them to DynamoDB as well?
2. What release version it should be? 0.2.2 or 0.3.0?

```
for dict1 in output_dict.values():
```

issue_title: Windows / Appveyor - same version of cached file - invalidated and built  again
issue_body:
I am using `pip-accel` with appveyor in my project and `pip-accel` invalidates even the same version of a package that is already cached:
```
pip_accel.bdist    INFO Invalidating old numpy (1.10.1) binary (source is newer) ..
pip_accel.bdist    INFO Building numpy (1.10.1) binary distribution ..
```
Url for appveyor build: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/matysek/pyinstaller-ewwmo/build/181/job/6aj9u6f8lsfbpej9

In the build appveyor is instructed to cache these paths:
```
C:\Users\appveyor\AppData\Local\pip
C:\Users\appveyor\AppData\Roaming\.pip-accel
```

* I've seen already similar issue when porting `pip-accel` to windows.
* My workaround was to ignore the `mtime`.
* It looks like `mtime` is not reliable when appveyor caches some files to any external storage between builds.

```
copyright = '2016, Zalando SE'
```
while `LICENSE` says `Copyright (c) 2015 Compose, Zalando SE` - probably both should be harmonized, and (for Zalando SE) extended to 2017.

issue_title: Postgresql.write_recovery_conf: FileNotFoundError
issue_body:
Migrated from https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/436

# Scenario

- Kubernetes 1.6.0 on GKE
- wal-e backups and basebackups stored on GCS
- env `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` points to account with GCS "Storage Object Admin" role.
- StatefulSet running spilo with patroni 1.2.5, postgresql 9.6, `replicas: 2`
- Empty volume mounted as `/home/postgres/pgdata`
- When creating the second pod cluster with leader, i.e. the error occurs when trying to create a new replica.

# Expected result

patroni should start downloading from basebackup or wal-e from GCS backup in order to not put additional strain on the master node, then start replicating from master. This seems to be supported by patroni, at least in an Kubernetes+AWS

# Actual result

```
2017-04-26 06:30:57,744 INFO: Lock owner: patroni-set-0006-0; I am patroni-set-0006-1
2017-04-26 06:30:57,751 INFO: trying to bootstrap from leader 'patroni-set-0006-0'

        DETAIL: The subcommand is "backup-list".
        STRUCTURED: time=2017-04-26T06:30:58.362701-00 pid=7246
oauth2client.transport INFO     Attempting refresh to obtain initial access_token
oauth2client.client INFO     Refreshing access_token
2017-04-26 06:30:59,064 INFO: replica has been created using wal_e
2017-04-26 06:30:59,064 INFO: bootstrapped from leader 'patroni-set-0006-0'
2017-04-26 06:30:59,068 ERROR: Exception during execution of long running task bootstrap from leader 'patroni-set-0006-0'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/async_executor.py", line 39, in run
    wakeup = func(*args) if args else func()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/ha.py", line 134, in clone
    return self.state_handler.follow(node_to_follow, cluster.leader, True)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/postgresql.py", line 1007, in follow
    return self._do_follow(primary_conninfo, leader, recovery, timeout)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/postgresql.py", line 1063, in _do_follow
    self.write_recovery_conf(primary_conninfo)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/postgresql.py", line 899, in write_recovery_conf
    with open(self._recovery_conf, 'w') as f:
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/home/postgres/pgdata/pgroot/data/recovery.conf'
```
repeated, as patroni is restarted by supervisor.

## Analysis

### Files and directories

From the running postgres-set-0006-1 pod.

#### patroni configuration

- `/home/postgres/postgres.yml` has been created: https://gist.github.com/joar/321652e961754d8362195b3776692185

#### wal-e configuration

```
/home/postgres/etc/wal-e.d/env/WALE_GS_PREFIX: gs://my-postgres-backup/spilo/my-scope/wal/
/home/postgres/etc/wal-e.d/env/GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS: /etc/patroni/secrets/service-account.json
/home/postgres/etc/wal-e.d/env/TMPDIR: /home/postgres/pgdata/tmp
```
`/etc/patroni/secrets/service-account.json` exists and is walid

#### Files in `data_dir`

`/home/postgres/pgdata` exists, but without `pgroot/data`
```
pgdata/
├── lost+found
├── pgroot
│   └── pg_log
│       ├── postgresql-0.csv
│       ├── postgresql-1.csv
│       ├── postgresql-2.csv
│       ├── postgresql-3.csv

│       ├── postgresql-5.csv
│       ├── postgresql-6.csv
│       └── postgresql-7.csv
└── tmp
```

### Trying to create `data_dir` manually

If I create `/home/postgres/pgdata/pgroot/data` manually, I get another error:

```
2017-04-26 09:33:53,787 INFO success: patroni entered RUNNING state, process has stayed up for > than 1 seconds (startsecs)
2017-04-26 09:33:53,847 INFO exited: patroni (exit status 1; not expected)

2017-04-26 09:33:55,353 CRITICAL: system ID mismatch, node patroni-set-0006-1 belongs to a different cluster: 6412988290203516990 != 
2017-04-26 09:33:55,858 ERROR: 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/__init__.py", line 134, in patroni_main
    patroni.run()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/__init__.py", line 110, in run
    logger.info(self.ha.run_cycle())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/ha.py", line 946, in run_cycle
    info = self._run_cycle()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/ha.py", line 903, in _run_cycle
    sys.exit(1)
SystemExit: 1

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/dcs/etcd.py", line 329, in wrapper
    retval = func(self, *args, **kwargs) is not None
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/dcs/etcd.py", line 511, in delete_leader
    return self._client.delete(self.leader_path, prevValue=self._name)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/etcd/client.py", line 584, in delete
    self.key_endpoint + key, self._MDELETE, params=kwds)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/patroni/dcs/etcd.py", line 211, in api_execute
    return self._handle_server_response(response)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/etcd/client.py", line 928, in _handle_server_response
```

caused by `self.state_handler.sysid` being `''` at [patroni/ha.py#L901](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/1d513e7e04cc0c69113b9a94d37b0296ceae2906/patroni/ha.py#L901).

The `Compare failed : [patroni-set-0006-1 != patroni-set-0006-0]` is curious, does my replica think it's the leader?

issue_title: Implement possibility to mark some nodes as not participating in leader election.
Run pg_rewind for the former master that was shut down uncleanly (i.e. killed -9)
issue_body:
Currently, pg_rewind can only process the former master being shut down cleanly. This is useful for the case of split brain/connection loss between the former master and DCS, but In real life some of the shutdowns will happen because of the PostgreSQL crash, due to bad hardware or bugs.

What we could do is to start the node that crashed in a single user mode and issue a checkpoint before trying to do pg_rewind on it. We have to check, though, that no data loss occurs as a result.

issue_title: Déplacer les variables liées au logement du Ménage vers la Famille ?
issue_body:
Aujourd'hui, les variables liées au logement comme `loyer` ou `statut_occupation_logement` sont toutes déclarées pour l'entité Ménage.
Le raisonnement étant, je crois, qu'un ménage désigne des personnes qui vivent toutes sous le même toit, est donc que c'est l'entité "naturelle" pour décrire le logement.

Dans la pratique, je constate que ce choix, du moins côté prestation sociales, n'a que des inconvénients. Par exemple, parmi les formules qui utilisent ces variables (sur tout le périmètre openfisca-france), toutes **sans exception** castent leur valeur vers l'entité famille.

En pratique, cela ne facilite pas la contribution. Les entités sont un facteur de complexité pour les nouveau contributeurs. Introduire les idées de Familles et d'Individu est nécessaire pour coder les textes de lois, mais la différence entre une Famille et un Ménage est une subtilité conceptuellement non nécessaire pour la grande majorité des contributeurs, qui ont pourtant bien besoin d'accéder à des informations de bases telles que le loyer.

Je propose donc, dans la PR à venir sur le refactor de la gestion des entités, de migrer toutes les variables au logement vers l'entité Famille. Est-ce que ça vous paraît acceptable ? Est-ce qu'il me manque un élément qui pousserait vers le maintien dans l'entité Ménage ?

Merci ! 

@benjello @cbenz

issue_title: better argparse sorting/description
issue_body:
Argparse arguments currently stay together based upon nested level, but their ordering within a nested level remain somewhat random.  I'd suggest that non-required arguments get pushed to the bottom, and Nested arguments get pushed to the top. 

so if i had

```Python
    import argschema

    class NestedSchema(argschema.schemas.DefaultSchema):
        aint = argschema.fields.Int(required=False, default =5, description="a integer")
        bstr = argschema.fields.Str(required=True, description = "a string")

    class MySchema(argschema.ArgSchema):
        nest = argschema.fields.Nested(NestedSchema, required=True, description="nested schema")
        topint = argschema.fields.Int(required=True,description="a top integer")
        topintb = argschema.fields.Int(default=5,required=False,description="a top integer b")
        topintc = argschema.fields.Int(required=True,description="a top integer c")
        topintd = argschema.fields.Int(default=7,required=False,description="a top integer d")

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        mod = argschema.ArgSchemaParser(schema_type=MySchema)
```

presently i get

```Shell
    $ python test_schema.py --help
    usage: test_schema.py [-h] [--log_level LOG_LEVEL] [--nest.aint NEST.AINT]
                        [--nest.bstr NEST.BSTR] [--input_json INPUT_JSON]
                        [--topintc TOPINTC] [--topintb TOPINTB]
                        [--topintd TOPINTD] [--topint TOPINT]
                        [--output_json OUTPUT_JSON]

    optional arguments:
    -h, --help            show this help message and exit
    --log_level LOG_LEVEL
                            set the logging level of the module
    --nest.aint NEST.AINT
                            a integer
    --nest.bstr NEST.BSTR
                            a string
    --input_json INPUT_JSON
                            file path of input json file
    --topintc TOPINTC     a top integer c
    --topintb TOPINTB     a top integer b
    --topintd TOPINTD     a top integer d
    --topint TOPINT       a top integer
    --output_json OUTPUT_JSON
                            file path to output json file
```
where I think i would prefer if the output were something like this

```Shell
    $ python test_schema.py --help
        usage: test_schema.py [-h] [--log_level LOG_LEVEL] [--nest.aint NEST.AINT]
                            [--nest.bstr NEST.BSTR] [--input_json INPUT_JSON]
                            [--topintc TOPINTC] [--topintb TOPINTB]
                            [--topintd TOPINTD] [--topint TOPINT]
                            [--output_json OUTPUT_JSON]

        optional arguments:                       
        --nest.aint NEST.AINT      a integer (default = 5)                        
        --nest.bstr NEST.BSTR      a string (required)       
        --topint TOPINT            a top integer (required)                   
        --topintc TOPINTC          a top integer c (required)
        --topintb TOPINTB          a top integer b (default=5)
        --topintd TOPINTD          a top integer d (default=7)
        --input_json INPUT_JSON    file path of input json file 
        --output_json OUTPUT_JSON  file path to output json file
        --log_level LOG_LEVEL      set the logging level of the module (default=WARNING)
        -h, --help                 show this help message and exit 
```

issue_title: Re-add Lambda Functions 'context' parameter when using events
issue_body:
When using the new S3, SNS, or SQS decorators, Lambda Functions are no longer declared with the `context` parameter:

```python
@app.on_sns_message(topic='my-demo-topic')
def handle_sns_message(event):
    app.log.debug("Received message with subject: %s, message: %s",
                  event.subject, event.message)
```

As mentioned in #488, using error monitoring tools such as [Sentry](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry) which require the `context` parameter, will result in the deployed application to break during lambda function runtime, with an error like the one below:

```
wrapped() missing 1 required positional argument: 'context': TypeError
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/var/task/chalice/app.py", line 989, in __call__
return self.func(event_obj)
TypeError: wrapped() missing 1 required positional argument: 'context'
```

I was wondering if it's possible to re-introduce `context` as a parameter. Even if it's really not relevant to the function itself. 

Would like some opinions.

issue_title: Nonuniform state numbers
issue_body:
When manticore forks, state id's are not assigned in a uniform, incremental way. In the below run of `examples/linux/basic`, we can see that the state ids are 0, 3, and 5, when they should intuitively be 0, 1, and 2. This makes the state id and incorrect source for the actual number of states that were created.

This happens because

1. In the State ctor, `get_new_id` is always called. When state are loaded (unpickled), this ctor executes, incrementing the global state count unnecessarily.
2. In State `__enter__` (copying mechanism), `get_new_id` is explicitly called, in addition to the ctor, which unnecessarily bumps the state count.

There are fixes for these in the dev-events branch, as a result of refactoring this and moving state counting to the executor.

issue_title: asizeof does not properly size namedtuples
issue_body:
If I run asizeof on a normal tuple, I get the following:

```
>>> from pympler import asizeof
>>> asizeof.asizeof(list(range(1000)), "foo")
41160
```

If I create a named tuple and run asizeof on that object, I don't get the correct size:

```
>>> from pympler import asizeof
>>> from collections import namedtuple
>>> TestTuple = namedtuple("TestTuple", ["a", "b"])
>>> asizeof.asizeof(TestTuple(list(range(1000)), "foo"))
120
```

I believe asizeof should be returning roughly the same values for these calls.

This output was produced on Python 3.4 using pympler 0.4.3. Perhaps this is related to #10?

issue_title: dealias_fourdd gives incorrect results or errors out when given a radar in which
issue_body:
The `dealias_fourdd` function will give incorrect results or raises a ValueError when passed a radar instances in which the sweeps limits do no incorporate all of the rays in the volume (one or more rays are not included in any sweeps, typical when rays "in-transition" are included in the volume).  

A minimal example is as follows:

``` Python
import pyart
import numpy as np
radar = pyart.testing.make_velocity_aliased_radar()
radar.sweep_start_ray_index['data'][0] = 1
height = np.linspace(150, 250, 10).astype('float32')
speed = np.ones((10), dtype='float32') * 0.5
direction = np.ones((10), dtype='float32') * 5.
pyart.correct.dealias_fourdd(
    radar, sounding_heights=height, sounding_wind_speeds=speed,
    sounding_wind_direction=direction, keep_original=True)
```

Which produces:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "minimal_bug.py", line 11, in <module>
    sounding_wind_direction=direction, keep_original=True)
  File "/home/jhelmus/dev/pyart/pyart/correct/dealias.py", line 250, in dealias_fourdd
    data = np.where(is_bad_data, vel_array, data)
ValueError: operands could not be broadcast together with shapes (359,50) (360,50) (359,50)
```

This can be fixed by creating a new velocity data array which is completely masked and filling in dealiased data for each sweep.

This bug was originally discovered by @oue in #421.

issue_title: Getting a ResponseNotReady() when reusing a request that requires a token
issue_body:
Hello, 

I'm not too sure if i'm doing this right. I followed [this documentation](http://requests-oauthlib.readthedocs.org/en/latest/oauth2_workflow.html#legacy-application-flow) and this [example](http://requests-oauthlib.readthedocs.org/en/latest/examples/github.html) when setting up my work. 

I wanted to use the username-password flow to obtain an access token which translates to the `LegacyApplicationClient`

I managed to get everything set up properly and do a request to a bearer-token secured endpoint. However, I get a `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: ('Connection aborted.', ResponseNotReady())` when repeating the request using the OAuth2Session client. The first time, the request comes back with the correct response. 

I can use python-requests normally and repeat those requests once I have my token but I cannot seem to repeat requests on the OAuth2Session secured client. Any ideas what the issue may be?

Here is my example code:

```
import requests
from oauthlib.oauth2 import LegacyApplicationClient
from requests_oauthlib import OAuth2Session
from requests.auth import HTTPBasicAuth

# since we are using http instead of https
import os
os.environ['OAUTHLIB_INSECURE_TRANSPORT'] = '1'

# Resource owner related
resource_owner_username = 'bob'
resource_owner_password = '1111'

offerOAuth = OAuth2Session(client=LegacyApplicationClient(client_id=client_app_id))

# Obtain a token with read all and write all privileges
# Manually refresh/(obtain for first time) token
token = offerOAuth.fetch_token(
    token_url=oauth_token_url,
    username=resource_owner_username,
    password=resource_owner_password,
    client_id=client_app_id,
    client_secret=client_app_secret,
    scope=['read.all', 'write.all'],
    verify=False,    # do not verify SSL certificate
)

print 'Token:', token['access_token']

# Request to resource owner endpoint through OAuth library
## Note: this cannot be repeated, gives me requests.exceptions.ConnectionError: ('Connection aborted.', ResponseNotReady())
for i in range(1,10):
    response = offerOAuth.get(resource_owner_endpoint, verify='False', timeout=10)
    print response.content


## Request to resource owner endpoint manually
## This can be repeated fine
for i in range(1,10):

        url='http://localhost:8080/targets?memberId=me',
        headers={'Authorization': ('Bearer ' + token['access_token'])}
    )
```

issue_title: Automatic token refresh on error
issue_body: Sometimes token can be revoked or otherwise made unusable on servers for various reasons without client knowing. It would be nice if `request()` could intercept such a specific error (recognizing it by status code and/or error code in the body, both configurable in arguments) and automatically attempt to refresh the token and retry (once) before failing.

issue_title: Ensure that gRPC metrics headers are in the order requested by the spec.
issue_body:
The present order in `api.core` is:

```
core/0.27.1 gl-python/3.6.0 grpc/0.27.1 gapic/0.27.2
```

The order per spec should be:

```
gl-python/2.7.12 gccl/0.21.0 gapic/0.1.0 core/0.14.1 grpc/1.0.1
```

issue_title: cloudfront does not allow for resource level permissions
issue_body:
When creating policies for cloudfront, resource level specifications are [not allowed](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_aws-services-that-work-with-iam.html) and [example](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?messageID=755009&tstart=0). So when creating policies the creator has to remember to change
```
"Resource": [
    "arn:aws:cloudfront:::*"
]
```
to 
```
"Resource": [
    "*"
]
```
for the policy to be correct.

issue_title: Test caching should only be enabled when using `--chroot`.
issue_body: As pointed out by @stuhood, for both `JUnitRun` (via #4823) and `PytestRun` (via #5033), test caching should probably only occur when `--chroot` is turned on; otherwise, tests could pass when the test or code under test reaches out to un-declared file deps. If those file deps change, the cache will not invalidate and breaks could sneak in silently behind an improperly cached success run.

issue_body:

To paper over the problem, I'm having to change this exclusion in ci.sh:

```
+++ b/build-support/bin/ci.sh
@@ -211,7 +211,7 @@ if [[ "${skip_testprojects:-false}" == "false" ]]; then
     # The following two targets lose out due to a resource collision, because `example_b` happens
     # to be first in the context, and test.junit mixes all classpaths.
     testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_a/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplea:examplea
-    testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_c/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplec:examplec
+    testprojects/maven_layout/resource_collision/example_b/src/test/java/org/pantsbuild/duplicateres/examplec:exampleb
   )
```

This was also recently changed by  @stuhood when he added a new test in this commit: 

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/commit/d2722aca13472981f2b1eb9cfb562696b8b97d63

```
commit d2722aca13472981f2b1eb9cfb562696b8b97d63
Author: Stu Hood <stuhood@gmail.com>
Date:   Sun Apr 26 17:34:15 2015 -0700

    Only add resources to the relevant target
```

I'm not sure if this commit caused a problem  - these tests really shouldn't ever be run together, at least not until we have an isolated test strategy like we have for the compile tasks.

issue_title: Reify Task-level invalidation, and isolate on-disk artifacts
issue_body:
As Task implementations and inputs change, their behaviour can change drastically. To account for this, we should expand the identity of a Task to include more than just its name, and include the additional information in Target-level hashes.

See https://groups.google.com/d/topic/pants-devel/4M89vjY94hE/discussion

issue_title: The leveled predicate change to build graph introduced some overtraversals
issue_body: We have a target with many overlapping edges that experiences superlinear slowdowns over the previous behavior. I'm working on a fix that maintains the existing observable behavior while reducing the number of unnecessary traversals.

issue_title: Bootstrap the native engine from live sources in the pants repo.
issue_body: When we release alpha, beta, prod builds of pants with the new native engine, we can always release 2 logical sets of binaries - the native binaries to bintray 1st, and then the python dists to pypi (with the `pantsbuild.pants` dist configured to point to a released native engine version). When developing though, we'll want to run the native engine from local sources directly just like we do with pants itself. Fixup `./pants` to ensure the native engine binary is pulled from a local up to date source built from live sources.
