issue_title: CFFI archives on pypi not compatible with OSX
issue_body:
If pants' own requirements are resolved against pypi, they will (occasionally?) result in `cp27mu` interpreter (whereas OSX usually requires `cp27u`, according to @kwlzn).

This results in errors like the following:
```
E       ImportError: dlopen(src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so, 2): Symbol not found: _PyUnicodeUCS4_AsASCIIString
E         Referenced from: src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so
E         Expected in: flat namespace
E        in src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so
```
... note: `cp27mu`.

A workaround is to nuke the
```
~/.cache/pants/python_cache
```
directory, and then retry the resolve.

----

The full solution likely involves adding support for filtering ABIs via https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/issues/303

issue_title: Add functionality to capture stdout in tests
issue_body:
In `clldutils.testing` we should add functionality to capture `stdout`, along the lines of
```python
import sys
from contextlib import contextmanager

from six import StringIO

@contextmanager
def capture(func, *args, **kw):
    out, sys.stdout = sys.stdout, StringIO()
    func(*args, **kw)
    sys.stdout.seek(0)
    yield sys.stdout.read()
    sys.stdout = out
```

issue_title: Transformer configuration example
issue_body:
First of all, nice work!
I'm currently playing with sockeye and I successfully trained a simple RNN with 3 layers and got really nice perplexities out of it.  I'm now trying to train a transformer (encoder & decoder) on the same data but I can't get nearly the same perplexity.  RNN's perplexity started a ~400 and quickly dropped to ~4 within a couple of updates and eventually ended ~1.3.  My Transformer run started at ~700 and is now stuck at ~270 which is far from ~1.3.  I'm not expecting both models to get to the same perplexity but at least in the same range.

I searched the net to find an example of a transformer configuration for sockeye and couldn't find any.  Would it be possible to have an example of minimum/good enough configuration of transformer in sockeye?

Here's my current sockeye command line:
```
python \
   -m sockeye.train \
      --source $corpora/train_en.bpe \
      --target $corpora/train_fr.bpe \
      --validation-source $corpora/dev1_en.bpe \
      --validation-target $corpora/dev1_fr.bpe \
      --encoder transformer \
      --decoder transformer \
      --num-layers 6:6 \
      --max-seq-len 60 \
      --decode-and-evaluate 500 \
      --decode-and-evaluate-use-cpu \
      --metrics perplexity \
      --optimizer adam \
      --use-tensorboard \
      -o model \
   2> log.train
```
Should I had residual connection ?

Thanks for your help.

Suggestion: I think it would be quite useful to have an option that take a yaml file as a configuration.  Having a way to pass a full configuration of sockeye via a yaml file would make experimentation easier and would also make sharing sockeye configuration easier.

issue_title: Incorrect table parsing
issue_body:
I've got an HTML document that contains some text inside of a table row: ```html foo
``` It looks like the text gets pulled outside of the table element for some reason (not sure how to print it with normal HTML-style syntax, but you can still see that `foo` has been moved): ```python >>> import html5lib >>> import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET >>> ET.tostring(html5lib.parse('foo
')) b'foo' ``` This is despite the example parsing correctly when using just `xml.etree`: ```python >>> import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET >>> ET.tostring(ET.ElementTree(ET.fromstring('foo

issue_title: __init__() takes at most 4 positional arguments (6 given) raised when using GoogleServiceAccountClient
issue_body:
When you create a GoogleServiceAccountClient object, a warning is logged to stderr like so: __init__() takes at most 4 positional arguments (6 given). This is because there is a positional arguments decorator on the oauth2client.client.SignedJwtAssertionCredentials method which the GoogleServiceAccountClient calls on init.

If the __init__ decorator on the class SignedJwtAssertionCredentials is raised from 4 to 6, this warning will disappear. The other option is to name more arguments that are being passed when calling oauth2client.client.SignedJwtAssertionCredentials in the GoogleServiceAccountClient initialisation.

issue_title: ASN in OPEN (23456) did not match ASN expected
Socket.sock invalid value
issue_body:
According to `nn_socket` man page, the function returns file descriptor, so `Socket.sock` attribute should reflect this.

If we run
```
import nnpy 

sub = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.PUB)
sub.bind('tcp://*:5555')
print(sub.sock)
```
The `0` will be printed.

`strace` gives the following in its output for above script
```
$ grep -e ^socket -e ^bind log           
socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM|SOCK_CLOEXEC, IPPROTO_IP) = 7
bind(7, {sa_family=AF_INET, sin_port=htons(5555), sin_addr=inet_addr("0.0.0.0")}, 16) = 0
```
So `Socket.sock` should be `7`, not `0`.

In file build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/_nnpy.c we can see
```
  { "nn_socket", (void *)_cffi_f_nn_socket, _CFFI_OP(_CFFI_OP_CPYTHON_BLTN_V, 14), (void *)_cffi_d_nn_socket },
```

Is that an issue with generator?

issue_title: use correct ssl version with urllib3 and pyopenssl
issue_body:
There are situations where ``get_max_tls_protocol()`` will get a max openssl version from python ``ssl`` that is higher than what ``urllib3`` supports.

The error will be:

```
KeyError: 5
```
in ``requests/packages/urllib3/contrib/pyopenssl.py:271`` on the line with ``ctx = OpenSSL.SSL.Context(_openssl_versions[ssl_version])``

Using the following replacement for ``docker.ssladapter.ssladapter.get_max_tls_protocol()`` worked for me, but there might be other cases I am missing:

```python
from requests.packages.urllib3.contrib import pyopenssl
def get_max_tls_protocol():
    protocols = ('PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2',
                 'PROTOCOL_TLSv1_1',
                 'PROTOCOL_TLSv1',
    for proto in protocols:
        if hasattr(ssl, proto):
            proto_id = getattr(ssl, proto)
            if proto_id in pyopenssl._openssl_versions:
                return proto_id
```

issue_title: Is it ok to have all zero grads sometimes?
issue_body:
Consider a case, where you have some RNN that embeds a sentence that consists of only one word.
In the simplest case, it has two sets of parameters: input2hidden, and hidded2hidden. In our case we have only one timestep, so no grads are computed for hidden2hidden parameters, and thus the test below will fail, despite the behavior is intended. https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4cda858012261c5fa5f668236abaeb4333d587ec/allennlp/common/testing/model_test_case.py#L119-L121

issue_title: Use flocker to replace Ceph for database & builder & ...
issue_body:
https://github.com/ClusterHQ/flocker

> Flocker is an open-source Container Data Volume Manager for your Dockerized applications.
> By providing tools for data migrations, Flocker gives ops teams the tools they need to run containerized stateful services like databases in production.
> Unlike a Docker data volume which is tied to a single server, a Flocker data volume, called a dataset, is portable and can be used with any container, no matter where that container is running.
> Flocker manages Docker containers and data volumes together. When you use Flocker to manage your stateful microservice, your volumes will follow your containers when they move between different hosts in your cluster.
> You can also use Flocker to manage only your volumes, while continuing to manage your containers however you choose.

issue_title: Proposal: refactor units in Go
issue_body:
Basically rewrite the current implementation in Go :)

This is just to improve the current output from the services

from:
```console
[Service]
EnvironmentFile=/etc/environment
TimeoutStartSec=30m
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "docker inspect deis-builder-data >/dev/null 2>&1 || docker run --name deis-builder-data -v /var/lib/docker ubuntu-debootstrap:14.04 /bin/true"
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/builder` && docker history $IMAGE >/dev/null || docker pull $IMAGE"
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "docker inspect deis-builder >/dev/null && docker rm -f deis-builder || true"
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/builder` && docker run --name deis-builder --rm -p 2223:22 --volumes-from=deis-builder-data -e EXTERNAL_PORT=2223 -e HOST=$COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 --privileged $IMAGE"
ExecStartPost=/bin/sh -c "echo 'Waiting for builder on 2223/tcp...' && until echo 'dummy-value' | ncat $COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 2223 >/dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 1; done"
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c "nsenter --pid --uts --mount --ipc --net --target $(docker inspect --format='{{ .State.Pid }}' deis-builder) /usr/local/bin/push-images"
ExecStopPost=-/usr/bin/docker rm -f deis-builder
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
```

to:
```console
[Service]
EnvironmentFile=/etc/environment
TimeoutStartSec=30m
ExecStartPre=/opt/bin/launch-data-container -name=deis-builder
ExecStartPre=/opt/bin/check-image -image=/deis/builder
ExecStartPre=/opt/bin/remove-running-container -name=deis-builder
ExecStart=/opt/bin/launch-container -image=/deis/builder -name=deis-builder
ExecStartPost=/opt/bin/wait-for-port -port=2223/tcp -text="Waiting for builder on 2223/tcp..."
ExecStartPost=/bin/bash -c "nsenter --pid --uts --mount --ipc --net --target $(docker inspect --format='{{ .State.Pid }}' deis-builder) /usr/local/bin/push-images"
ExecStopPost=/opt/bin/stop-container -name=deis-builder
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
```

Every ExecStartPre line is just a small go app doing the same thing than before.
Why different apps? To keep it simple, 1 app -> 1 task

In ExecStart:
```console
ExecStart=/opt/bin/launch-container -image=/deis/builder -name=deis-builder
````

**Objectives:**

* Encapsulate the details
* Avoid shell mess
* use etcd to customize the service:
```
/deis/systemd/deis-builder/
                           variables
                           volumes
                           ports
```

doing this is possible to do other things:
```
/deis/systemd/app/defaults/
                           variables
                           volumes
/deis/systemd/app/example-php/
                           variables                                                                  
````

*In variables is possible to common custom variables without touching deis-controller python template*



* swallow the output.

  Show just what is necessary, things like `Unable to find image ubuntu-debootstrap:14.04 locally` are useless to new users.

* show more details

  Provide a way to get more details (something like in https://github.com/deis/deis/pull/2203) using /deis/debugMode to indicate this

* Add /deis/platform/useNodeNames (-h %H)

  Use the real node hostname inside the container (and not a generated one)


**Here is where I'm going to write the prototype https://github.com/aledbf/systemd-go**

issue_title: Rewrite components in Go so they are resilient, testable, and self-publishing
issue_body:
There's been some discussion and debate in #2701 about how to approach refactoring components. The core maintainers had an architectural meeting today and decided to move forward with a Go-based rewrite of the components, with the following goals:
- Better log output for components, showing the component's actual status and helpful details when it fails (no more `waiting for confd to write initial templates`)
- Components are more easily testable and less fragile (such as builder)
- confd runs in-memory, enabling us to vendor some of its code so we can use native templating logic
- Shared Go-based libraries (with unit tests!) for code common among boot scripts
- Cleaner code and improved control flow made possible by use of Go idioms and channels (for example, rewriting the SSL certificate generation logic)

A long-term goal here is to at some point in the future be able to say `docker run -P deis/builder:2.0` and have the component come up and publish itself with everything it needs self-contained.

**Implementation details**
- Do we use go-extpoints or not? An argument against is that it lends itself well to plugins, but that may not be as useful in this implementation

replaces #2701

issue_title: Store and use access tokens in the configuration file
issue_body:
Currently every command invocation fetches a new access token from the stored refresh token. I think this is going to quickly get out of hand if and when people start making a lot of CLI calls.

Storing access tokens (and their expiration time) in the configuration file will allow us to make calls directly instead of needing to always go and get a token. Doing this should have the added benefit of making each CLI command more responsive.

I'm thinking we add the following new keys:

`<service>_access_token`
`<service>_access_token_expires_at`

issue_title: Add Task Wait
issue_body:
It would be nice to have something similar to the old CLI `wait` command.

The idea is to be a blocking CLI command which returns with status 0 once the Task terminates.
Importantly, it should exit with status 0 even if the Task itself fails.
The exit code is necessary to distinguish network failure (likely on a long-running command) from successful termination.

Should let you write something like this:

```shell
#!/bin/sh
...
rc=1
while [ $rc -ne 0 ];
do
    # wait 30m at a time
    globus transfer task wait --task-id '...' --timeout 1800
    echo 'still alive and polling' >2
    rc=$?
done
```

Some thoughts on what we might want for this, interface-wise:

Command Name
---
`globus transfer task wait`.
It's a task-oriented feature, so it makes sense as a subcommand of `globus transfer task`.

Options
---
`--timeout`
This is not strictly necessary, but a waiting process probably wants to heartbeat periodically to show that it's still alive. After N seconds, exit with status 0 if the task is still pending.

`--polling-interval`
Check the status every N seconds; defaults to 1.
If you plan to poll many tasks simultaneously, you want to be able to reduce network traffic by lengthening the interval between queries.
If you want to watch a task at very high resolution, you'll still be limited by RTT for a given request.
We could do higher resolution polling by running multiple parallel requests, but that seems excessive.

`--fail-on-fault-types`
This is the most tenuous potential flag -- seems somewhat fragile.
If there are fault events of a particular type in the Task events, it may be desirable to consider it failed. Particularly, I'm thinking of Permission Denied or nonexistent dest dirs.
This would be an enum of string options, passed as a comma delimited list, defaulting to the empty list.

issue_title: `task event-list` has a bad text output format
issue_body:
Right now, it tries to table-print its results. However, one of the columns it's printing is an arbitrary JSON blob, so it doesn't fit into the tabular format.
Furthermore, the table printing logic tries to use the maximum length of that field as the width of the column, which makes for a bad header as well.

We should probably do something other than the tabular format.
I can't think of a way of making that table work.

Ideally, it would be consistent in look and feel with the other non-tabular output which we produce.

issue_title: Failing to guess a single MS-apostrophe
issue_body:
I have a page of text in ASCII with a single Microsoft-apostrophe `chr(8217)` detected as **ISO-8859-2**. 
```python
#1. Create problematic sample
>>> s = 'today' + chr(8217) + 's research'
>>> s
'today’s research'
>>> b = s.encode('windows-1252')
>>> b
b'today\x92s research'

#2. Attempt to decode it
>>> chardet.detect(b)
{'encoding': 'ISO-8859-2', 'confidence': 0.8060609643099236}
>>> b.decode('ISO-8859-2')
'today\x92s research'

#3. Now try the correct encoding
>>> b.decode('windows-1252')
'today’s research'
```
This text is very typical of anything created using a Microsoft editor. Furthermore, latest version of Firefox detects it correctly. I am using Python 3.3. Any help is appreciated.

issue_title: Private key signing not possible
issue_body:
* Version: 3.7.2
* Python: 3.5
* OS: ubuntu 16.04 lts


### What was wrong?

When following instructions on http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/managers.html for private key signing, if the transaction 

`web3.eth.sendTransaction({'from':web3.eth.accounts[1],'to':web3.eth.accounts[0],'value':1000000000000})`

is sent, the result in the geth node is

`Tx(044891110df9790252c41bd6f1af0b0b7f1cfac8eea3ccc8d8b7f8ccfe0141c7) to: &7361cf89b8ea0a4224a8330df0ade1130f3a2192`

whereas the correct one: 

`Tx(0x45ed38ec7e8b8e6f86f6d1b279736ad4f45dd8336011289a1017d0a0f01b6e37) to: 0x7361cf89b8ea0a4224a8330df0ade1130f3a2192`

is printed if exact the same transaction is called with standard wallet manager.

issue_title: Possibility to optimize IPCProvider.make_request
issue_body:
* Version: 4.2.1

### What was wrong?

In the current implementation the response will be parsed on every `recv` from the socket https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/providers/ipc.py#L175

In some performance critical tasks parsing JSON can become a bottleneck especially if the response is big

### How can it be fixed?

Is it possible to check the last bytes of the response first to see if they are a valid JSON RPC terminating character such as },] and only if true try to parse JSON?

issue_title: dependency cycle with py-evm backend
issue_body:
* Version: 0.1.0b23
* Python: 3.6
* OS: osx


### What was wrong?

I specify `eth-tester[py-evm]>=0.1.0-beta.23,<1.0` in my dependencies. `pip install -r requirements.txt` works fine with that.

However, when trying to run `python setup.py develop` on my package I get the following error:

```
error: eth-tester 0.1.0b23 is installed but eth-tester==0.1.0b21 is required by {'py-evm'}
```

eth-tester requires py-evm 0.2.0a14, but this one in return requires eth-tester 0.1.0b21: https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/cc877851d4e110eabfba1bfe544bfe187b51ebea/setup.py#L29

### How can it be fixed?

Fix the dependency cycle.

issue_title: Local package cache higher in priority than conda-build folders
issue_body:
If I have two recipes, "upstream" and "downstream", I see very undesirable behavior with conda.

1. build upstream - hash abc123, timestamp 
2. build downstream
3. change upstream, rebuild - hash (somethingnew)
4. build downstream

The second "downstream" build will use the initial "upstream" package, not the more recent build.  I suspect that it is because conda is finding the older version first in the pkgs folder.  I have not been able to track down where this happens.  Here's where I've traced it:

1. get_index: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/core/index.py#L39
2. get_channel_priority_map prepends "local": https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/core/index.py#L126
3. 'local' multichannel defined using conda_build_local_urls: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/301133cb8c804730c218ad8e47f116a8b1af51fc/conda/base/context.py#L453
4. conda_build_local_paths: https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/347b8e5d5c0f8798f950b07d08d9f0c81241843b/conda/base/context.py#L197

None of these show pkgs.  Where is this coming in?

issue_title: root environment is always writable
issue_body:
I'm assembling a [Spack](https://github.com/llnl/spack) package for miniconda.

I'd like to treat the resulting installation as "read only" -- in particular, environments installed by users shouldn't end up in the root environment.

This bit in the "envs_dirs" section of [the documentation about centralized installation](https://conda.io/docs/install/central.html) :
> Specify directories in which environments are located. If this key is set, the root prefix envs_dir is not used unless explicitly included. This key also determines where the package caches will be located.

tells me that if I set a value for `envs_dir` then the one in the root prefix will not be used.

But, this .condarc in the root environment directory:

```
envs_dirs:
  - ~/.conda/envs
```

produces this output in `conda info`:

```
...
       envs directories : /home/users/hartzelg/.conda/envs
                          /remote/home/hartzelg/tmp/spack/opt/spack/linux-centos7-x86_64/gcc-4.8.3/miniconda2-4.3.11-vseza2bw4ufem7htzv4jltbvxuzglbmq/envs
```

I've seen other mention that if the root environment is not writable then it is not included in envs directories list.

I have been unable to convince `conda` that the root env. directory is not writable.

I've chmod'ed the root directory itself to `555`.

Then I figured out that it was actually testing `.../conda-meta/history` so I chmod'ed that `444` and for good measure chmod'ed `.../conda-meta` to `555`.

```

        print("In info_dict, root_writable is: %s" %info_dict['root_writable'])

issue_title: Check all dependencies of conda with a test of a working conda
issue_body:
This is from https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/issues/666#issuecomment-221294317

Another suggestion: all packages which are in the dependency chain of conda should get a test which installs conda (not sure if that's possible in a test environment) and use it to install and remove/downgrade a package. That way the real disaster of installing a broken package into the root environment which disables conda itself could be prevented.

issue_title: "No such file or directory" during multiple parallel activations
issue_body:
Nearly identically to #2837, I am also working in a clustered environment where multiple parallel processes are activating the same env simultaneously and I am getting "No such file or directory" regularly.

In fact running the following script, in 3 different terminals at once, will reproduce the problem within a few seconds:

```
#!/bin/bash
# Look for race condition in activating an anaconda env
# Run this in multiple windows at once and wait for one of them to crash.

ret=0
while [ "$ret" == "0" ]; do
    echo -n .
    source activate myenv
    ret=$?
done
```

The immediate solution (as #2837 did) would appear to be to add another exception to the exception [in install.py in symlink_conda_hlp at ~line 616](https://github.com/kalefranz/conda/blob/8b5bc4ec76075f2896d5a53d1b5e41a0194a9c11/conda/install.py#L616).  This time adding errno.ENOENT to the list.

So many exceptions to exceptions raises the question in my mind if re-symlinking is the right thing to do during activation.   If the symlinks can't be entirely removed, could this be replaced with just a check if the symlinks are there and a warning if somehow they've gone missing?  Keeping it read-only will avoid these race conditions.  (In fact, the above suggestion probably doesn't remove all the possible race conditions here.)  An arg to activate to force re-symlinking could be added to override the default of not re-symlinking.  Just some thoughts...

CC'ing NERSC admins who have helped me debug this: @rcthomas

issue_title: binaries from sub-environ on different volume can't find shared libraries
issue_body:
When executing a binary installed in an environment created from a parent environment binaries with dependencies outside their package can't find their shared libraries.

Two examples of binaries that require libraries from other packages and are therefore broken:
- curl: requires openssl package
- ncdump (from libnetcdf): required hdf5 package

To reproduce:

```
$ which conda
/opt/anaconda/bin/conda

$ conda create -y -p ./env curl

$ source activate ./env
(/data/brucef/env)$ conda install -y curl

(/data/brucef/env)$ which curl
/data/brucef/env/bin/curl

(/data/brucef/env)$ ldd ./env/bin/curl | grep libssl
    libssl.so.1.0.0 => /data/brucef/./env/bin/../lib/libssl.so.1.0.0 (0x00007fc2af726000)

(/data/brucef/env)$ curl
curl: error while loading shared libraries: libssl.so.1.0.0: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

The problem seems to be that the RPATH is causing the lib loader to look for the library relative the actual binary path not the softlinked path:

```
(/data/brucef/env)$ ll ./env/bin/curl
lrwxrwxrwx 1 brucef domain users 53 Jan 11 23:17 ./env/bin/curl -> /home/brucef/.conda/envs/.pkgs/curl-7.45.0-0/bin/curl

(/data/brucef/env)$ readelf -a ./env/bin/curl | grep RPATH
 0x000000000000000f (RPATH)              Library rpath: [$ORIGIN/../lib]

(/data/brucef/env)$ LD_DEBUG=all curl
... snip ...
     23406:  search path=/home/brucef/.conda/envs/.pkgs/curl-7.45.0-0/bin/../lib        (RPATH from file curl)
     23406:   trying file=/home/brucef/.conda/envs/.pkgs/curl-7.45.0-0/bin/../lib/libssl.so.1.0.0
... snip ...
```

Seems like it should be using the path of the softlink `/data/brucef/env/bin/../lib/libssl.so.1.0.0`

issue_title: Avoid sending 'enable' for Node.run_commands() method
issue_body:
Since we offer Node.enable() to send an array of commands to the node in enable mode, it may be a bit more logical to move the enablement to the same.

Though it is preferred to use config() or enable(), we still allow the usage of run_commands() and enable() actually calls it, so it seems there is a separation that would make sense.

The reasoning here is that if you use command authorization, and you are already a privileged user, we will needlessly send 'enable' with every command.  This add latency and logs to the command authorization server get spammed.  

If a user tries to invoke run_commands() for a command that is not authorized, it should probably respond with an exception, and then they can decide to use the enable() method instead.

issue_title: Epoch option for age filter
issue_body:
I think the current setting of an epoch in the age filter (as a point of reference) is not very practicable. An automated task would have to read / update / write the yaml settings file if it tried to change the epoch.

My use case is that I that the epoch for my age filter needs to be changed frequently, i.e. "snapshot all indexes prior to date x". That date is calculated in a script and can change frequently. It would be nice to have a feature to pass the epoch through a command line flag, or at least pass the whole action.yml file as a string to the curator command line.

issue_title: Urllib3HttpConnection maxsize is not the actual max size
issue_body:
Per the urllib3 docs:

> **maxsize** – Number of connections to save that can be reused. More than 1 is useful in multithreaded situations. If block is set to False, more connections will be created but they will not be saved once they’ve been used.

This means that passing `maxsize` will not actually limit your pool to `maxsize` connections per host. That's just how many it keeps alive. If your pool is being exhausted, it will indefinitely spawn new connections and throw them away, as the default value of `block` is `False`. `Urllib3HttpConnection` currently does not expose the parameter directly, or the ability to indirectly pass it through `**kwargs` so there is no way to configure it.

issue_title: DNS cache in `aiohttp.TCPConnector` causes client requests to timeout when host address changes
issue_body:
Hi!

Using domain-based elasticsearch hosts that change their IP over time can cause `AsyncElasticsearch` client to timeout requests as the default DNS cache resolves the hostname with old address.
 
For example, with Elasticsearch Heroku Add-ons that run on AWS, the host URL doesn't change and looks like `https://abcdef-123455.eu-west-1.bonsaisearch.net`, but the IP address can change over time. Because of the DNS cache - which isn't flushed - the client tries to send requests to wrong host IP and failes.

Solution might be to pass explicit `use_dns_cache` param to `aiohttp.TCPConnector` [in the constructor of `AIOHttpConnection`](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py-async/blob/c76817dd24e4cfa0ced0fa13fa4a70721d649df0/elasticsearch_async/connection.py#L29). 
Additionally, because of the default DNS resolver being [thread-based](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/blob/9fbb7d708375e0ba01d435c1e8cf41912381c0fc/aiohttp/resolver.py#L17), a `dns_resolver` param could be used to specify own, for example `aiodns`-based [AsyncResolver](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/blob/9fbb7d708375e0ba01d435c1e8cf41912381c0fc/aiohttp/resolver.py#L47), although it was removed as default one because of compatibility [issues with IPv6 systems](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/issues/559).

I can submit a PR with this solution soon.
What do you think?

issue_title: Unhelpful NIC creation error (better help required?)
issue_body:
When creating a NIC, --subnet is a required argument and it says it takes a 'Name or ID of an existing subnet'.
However, if you provide a name, you must also pass in --vnet-name.

If you do not, you get an unhelpful error message.
```
$ az network nic create -g derek1 -n mynewnic --subnet dvm1Subnet --public-ip-address mynewpublicip
'new' is not valid value for enum <enum 'subnetType'>
```

The current help for the command is as follows:
```
$ az network nic create --help

Command
    az network nic create

Arguments
    --name -n           [Required]: Name of the new NIC.
    --resource-group -g [Required]: Name of resource group.
    --subnet            [Required]: Name or ID of an existing subnet.
    --internal-dns-name           : The internal DNS name label.
    --ip-forwarding               : Enable IP forwarding.
    --lb-address-pools            : Space separated list of names or IDs of load balancer address
                                    pools to associate with the NIC. If names are used, --lb-name
                                    must be specified.
    --lb-inbound-nat-rules        : Space separated list of names or IDs of load balancer inbound
                                    NAT rules to associate with the NIC. If names are used, --lb-
                                    name must be specified.
    --lb-name                     : The name of the load balancer to use when adding NAT rules or
                                    address pools by name (ignored when IDs are specified).
    --location -l                 : Location.
    --network-security-group      : Name or ID of an existing network security group to associate
                                    with the NIC.
    --private-ip-address          : Static private IP address to use.
    --private-ip-address-version  : The private IP address version to use.  Allowed values: ipv4,
                                    ipv6.  Default: ipv4.
    --public-ip-address           : Name or ID an existing public IP address to associate with the
                                    NIC.
    --tags                        : Multiple semicolon separated tags in 'key[=value]' format. Omit
                                    value to clear tags.
    --vnet-name                   : The virtual network (VNet) associated with the provided subnet
                                    name (Omit if supplying a subnet id).
```

Help for `--vnet-name` should instead say `(Required if subnet name instead of id supplied)`. This would be better than `(Omit if supplying a subnet id)` because asking the user to __omit__ the parameter when they likely would not have known to add it in the first place is a bit backwards IMO.

When I specifed subnet id instead of name, the NIC creation worked as expected.

issue_title: az ad sp create-for-rbac and the --name parameter
issue_body:
Hi,

When creating a SP for use with a Container Registry, I ran the following with the name **sfacr** (per [docs](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-get-started-azure-cli)):

```bash
az ad sp create-for-rbac --name sfacr --scopes /subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourcegroups/ContainerRegistry/providers/Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/sfacr --role Owner --password <password>

I expected the resulting SP to be named **sfacr**, and the URI to be something like **http://sfacr**. The response says that a SP was created named **sfacr**, however under closer inspection it is actually named **azure-cli-2016-12-19-20-42-08**.  

Looking at the `-h` documentation it does say that `--name` is for "An unique uri. If missing, the command will generate one" but that feels unnatural when throughout the CLI, `-n --name` is used for display names. 

Would it be better to use `-n` for the display name, and a `-u` or `--uri` to designate the App ID URI?

issue_title: VirtualMachineSizeTypes enum doesn't include H-series VMs

issue_body:
I'm fairly new to hmms, so forgive me if I sound ignorant.

Essentially, I'm creating input features by sampling from 3 normal distributions, all centred at 5.0, with a standard deviations of 0.0000001. I train the HMM using these observations, and then use the **sample** method to generate samples from my HMM. Then I use the **score** method to get an idea of the probability of this sequence in the model. 

Here's the code I am using:

```python
import math
import numpy as np
from hmmlearn import hmm

m, s = 5.0,0.0000001

g1 = np.random.normal(m, s, 1000)
g2 = np.random.normal(m, s, 1000)
g3 = np.random.normal(m, s, 1000)

X = np.column_stack([g1, g2, g3])
HMM = hmm.GaussianHMM(n_components=3, n_iter=1000)
HMM.fit(X)

for i in range(0,100):
    sample, _ = HMM.sample(1)
    print sample
    prob = HMM.score(sample)
    print 'log-like: ', prob, '   prob: ', math.exp(prob) 
```

issue_title: Make resolving a url easier
issue_body:
## Long story short

Assuming there's a resource `/foo/bar/`, I want (like in django) a request to `/foo/bar` to be redirected (301) to `/foo/bar/`. 

I don't want this to be built into aiohttp, it's very simple in middleware and belongs outside the main repo, however currently it involves calling numerous private methods.

## Expected behaviour

`request.app.router.resolve_str('/foo/bar/')` (or whatever) to return information about the associated resource

## Actual behaviour

My current solution is

```python
except HTTPException as e:
    if request.method == METH_GET and e.status == 404 and not request.rel_url.raw_path.endswith('/'):
        possible_path = request.rel_url.raw_path + '/'
        for resource in request.app.router._resources:
            match_dict = resource._match(possible_path)
            if match_dict:
                raise HTTPMovedPermanently(possible_path)
```

Note the use of private properties.

issue_title: Parser of the Forwarded header ignores the port number
issue_body:
## Long story short

Parser of the `Forwarded` header ignores the port number.

## Expected behaviour

Parsing the header `Forwarded: for=1.2.3.4:5678` should yield a `request.forwarded` value of `({"for": "1.2.3.4:5678"},)`.

## Actual behaviour

Parsing the header `Forwarded: for=1.2.3.4:5678` gives me `request.forwarded` value of `({"for": "1.2.3.4"},)`.  That is, it removes the client port number.

## Your environment

aiohttp 3.2.1 / Python 3.6.5.

issue_title: Running out of memory

There are 25000 clans with 50 players in each clan. I have written a [script](https://github.com/amir20/clashstats/blob/master/clashleaders/worker.py#L51) that fetches each clan and updates all stats for all players. However, after 7 hours, I ran out of memory and the process is restarted. I request about 60 http calls per second. ## Expected behaviour For the memory to be static over time. ## Actual behaviour Instead, the memory will be like a waterfall and eventually kill the process. I also have mongo on the same server which sometimes gets killed. ## Steps to reproduce This is my code that does the actual work. ```python import asyncio import logging import os from urllib.parse import quote import aiohttp import requests API_TOKEN = os.getenv('API_TOKEN') HEADERS = {'authorization': 'Bearer ' + API_TOKEN} logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) class ClanNotFound(Exception): pass async def __fetch(url, session): async with session.get(url) as response: return await response.json() def __get_all(urls): loop = asyncio.get_event_loop() jar = aiohttp.DummyCookieJar() with aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop, cookie_jar=jar, headers=HEADERS) as session: futures = [__fetch(url, session) for url in urls] responses = loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*futures)) return responses def fetch_all_players(clan): logger.info(f"Fetching all player stats for {clan['tag']}.") tags = [member['tag'] for member in clan['memberList']] urls = ['https://api.clashofclans.com/v1/players/' + quote(tag) for tag in tags] return __get_all(urls) ``` ## Your environment I am running `Python 3.6.4` in Docker on DigitalOcean with 2GB of RAM. I am using `client`. My Docker file is [here](https://github.com/amir20/clashstats/blob/master/Dockerfile). I don't know what else to try. I saw some memory related issues regarding HTTPS but it seems those have been fixed. How should I go about debugging this?

# Description

I wanted to check something in particular: what happens if you only monitor events and the DOCKER_HOST is set to something unreachable.

The script should not even start, it should notice that the connection cannot be made. Also it should exit cleanly.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import asyncio
from aiodocker.docker import Docker

    subscriber = docker.events.subscribe()

    while True:
        event = await subscriber.get()
        print(f"event: {event!r}")

    docker = Docker()

        # explicitly stop monitoring.
        event_task.cancel()
    finally:

issue_title: HTTP Proxy settings not passed to aiohttp request
issue_body:
HTTP proxy defined in *HTTP_PROXY* and *HTTPS_PROXY* environment variables are not passed to *aiohttp request*, which makes the library unusable in private VPCs for other things than S3 with VPC endpoint.

Looks like the proxy configuration is read using utils derived from *botocore* but then it's just ignored in the AioEndpoint constructor.

issue_title: Notebooks marked unsaved on opening.
issue_body:
This is new behavior with the latest release 4.1.0 I open a notebook, and at the top of the title bar it states 

    Last Checkpoint: a few minutes ago (unsaved changes)

I press the save button, and confirm that the terminal prints out a save message. The header changes to:

    Last Checkpoint: a few seconds ago (autosaved)

I close the notebook window, then reopen the notebook. The top of the title bar states

    Last Checkpoint: a few seconds ago (unsaved changes)

In short, every time I open a notebook it is marked as unsaved.

issue_title: Navigation feature request
issue_body:
Would it be possible to add a way to move the active cell *with the keyboard* by more than one cell at a time?  I.e. "scroll" the notebook but also move the active cell along with the view. Currently you can scroll around the notebook easily but only with the view - the selected cell doesn't change.  I don't know how feasible it is to make it match the browser scroll, but even just a keyboard shortcut that would "skip" N number of cells (maybe where N is configurable) would be *awesome*.

The use case - I open a long notebook with a lot of cells in it; some are markdown for labeling but most are code.  There is a section at the bottom that I want to go to right away.  Right now I have two options: 1) scroll down there with Space or PageDown, then use the mouse to click on the cell I want or 2) hit J or Down Arrow a gazillion times (in actuality probably ~80).  Obviously option 2 is really inconvenient and slow, and I *really* don't like doing option 1.

Some context - I have bad shoulder problems, and because of that I use the keyboard as much as is humanly possible because using the mouse actually hurts.  Not being able to jump around the notebook (**with the active cell, not just the view**) is the foremost issue I have with the Notebooks (which I otherwise love!).

If there is already a way to do this, please tell me how!  And I apologize if this is a duplicate or has been discussed and rejected ... searching for "scroll" or "focus" seemed to just turn up lots of stuff about scrolling within cells.

issue_title: High order mixed derivatives of a multivariate function?
issue_body:
That might be not only an autograd-related question, but just a general math that might have an auto-grad related solution.

Given a multivariate function $f(x0..xn)$ I wonder if there is a way to compute or approximate a mixed derivative wrt each variable? $ d f^n(x0..xn) / dx0 ... dxn $?

If one just computes the gradient wrt whole $x$ and then takes first component of it, then computes gradient of that, takes second component, etc. - seems to be too computationally expensive for n in the order of hundreds/thousands. Does anyone have a better suggestion on how to implement it more effectively using autograd or knows how to get an approximate value without computing derivative n times?

Thanks in advance!

Ben

issue_body:
From suggestions from issue #549, but also a number of other sources, we can improve the initial cli `setup` experience as follows:

After running
```
$ blockstack setup
<ENTER A NEW PASSWORD>
```

1. obfuscate password on "Got backup?" query; do not print out on terminal.
2. Note to user that config files have been installed to $HOME/.blockstack
3. Note to user that 'wallet.json' file in $HOME/.blockstack/ contains public key, payment address, and 'enc'

issue_title: Subdomains: allow any name to send subdomain updates for any existing subdomain
issue_body:
Right now, updates to a subdomain can only be done through the owner of the stem name.  That is, the only way to send an update to `jude.blockstack.id` is to send a `NAME_UPDATE` for `blockstack.id`.

This does not have to be the case.  Instead, *any* name ought to be able to send a `NAME_UPDATE` that incorporates an update for a subdomain.  This lets subdomain owners recover from lost or unresponsive name owners.

However, there are two constraints that have to be enforced:
* A subdomain must *originate* on its stem name.  That is, `jude.blockstack.id` has to be created by `blockstack.id`.  It cannot be created by `jude.blockstack2.id`.
* A subdoman will only be created if *all prior zone files* for the stem name are known in advance.  This is to prove the *absence* of any prior subdomain creation operation on this subdomain.  It cannot be the case that `jude.blockstack.id` gets registered twice; enforcing the above two constraints lets us do this.

issue_title: STACKS: What happens if we're missing an address in the genesis block?
issue_body:
@kantai, @jeffdomke, and @jessesoslow raised a critical concern for deploying the Stacks token:  what happens if, somehow, we don't get somebody's account address by the time the genesis block goes live?  This could happen if the token buyer loses their email address, or the token buyer dies before the genesis block launches and their estate executor doesn't know to send us an address.

The solution we arrived at was to create an `ACCOUNT_TRANSFER` opcode.  The `ACCOUNT_TRANSFER` opcode would do the following:
* Transfer all tokens from the sender address to the receiver address
* Transfer all token vesting schedules from the sender address to the receiver address (so the recipient would vest the tokens instead)
* Transfer the transfer lock-up period from the sender address to the receiver address (so the recipient cannot spend tokens until the lock-up period passes).
* Remove the sender address from the accounts table.

The `ACCOUNT_TRANSFER` operation could only be used **once** over the lifetime of an account.  Once an account is transferred, it cannot be transferred again.

When the genesis block launches, all accounts that have addresses would be non-transferrable by default.  Only accounts for which we **do not yet have an address for** would be transferrable.  We would hold onto a private key for each such account, and issue a `ACCOUNT_TRANSFER` transaction to send the account to the rightful owner once they reach out to us and give us an address.

@shea256 @muneeb-ali Thoughts or concerns?

issue_title: Discuss new CLI interface
issue_body:
In v0.15 we will revamp the CLI interface. A reminder that major versions are either (a) consensus breaking changes or (b) interface changes. Since this change breaks the last CLI interface, we'll jump from v0.14.x to v0.15. 

I'm starting this issue as a thread for discussing CLI commands in general. For reference here is the current list:

```
(blockstack)muneeb@amsterdam:Desktop$ blockstack
usage: blockstack [-h]
                  {balance,configure,deposit,import,info,lookup,migrate,names,ping,price,register,renew,revoke,set_advanced_mode,transfer,update,whois}
                  ...

Blockstack cli version 0.14.0.7

positional arguments:
  {balance,configure,deposit,import,info,lookup,migrate,names,ping,price,register,renew,revoke,set_advanced_mode,transfer,update,whois}
    balance             Get the account balance
    configure           Interactively configure the client
    deposit             Display the address with which to receive bitcoins
    import              Display the address with which to receive names
    info                Get details about pending name commands
    lookup              Get the zone file and profile for a particular name
    migrate             Migrate a profile to the latest profile format
    names               Display the names owned by local addresses
    ping                Check server status and get server details
    price               Get the price of a name
    register            Register a name
    renew               Renew a name
    revoke              Revoke a name
    set_advanced_mode   Enable advanced commands
    transfer            Transfer a name to a new address
    update              Set the zone file for a name
    whois               Look up the blockchain info for a name

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
{}
```

There is also an advanced mode, which we can go over after the basic operations are fleshed out.

I can get feedback from everyone, try to reach consensus on what the next interface should look like, and lock-down the description and get sign off. We'll close this issue when we have the new interface locked down.

We'll support the new interface for at least a year (meaning Spring 2018) if not longer. So now is a good time to send any comments/concerns!

issue_title: Support form copy
Add links to explain how to test the code
Feature maps extracted from replays have digits out of range
issue_body:
I am trying to extract feature maps from replays to conduct supervise learning, in this procedure, i found some extracted features are out of range.
in pysc2/lib/features.py line154
```
SCREEN_FEATURES = ScreenFeatures(
    ...
    unit_type=(1850, FeatureType.CATEGORICAL, colors.unit_type, False),
    ...
)
```
which means **screen feature: unit_type** is categorical and bounded by 1850, so we should do data pre-processing as the paper [SC2LE](https://arxiv.org/abs/1708.04782) described in section 4.3:

> **Input pre-processing** All the baseline agents share the same pre-processing of input feature layers.
We **_embed all feature layers containing categorical values into a continuous space which is
equivalent to using a one-hot encoding in the channel dimension followed by a 1 x 1 convolution_**.
We also re-scale numerical features with a logarithmic transformation as some of them such as
hit-points or minerals might attain substantially high values

But after i extracted replay 

> 7c84df5f0454257f5ebd07cb60ab405fb4062f3df80ab3a16a3f68e2c78602b5.SC2Replay

I found the max value of this screen_feature/unit_type is 1879, which is larger than 1850
Also, another replay

> 39c23cd78c1ea5313f21bd2fba393733208bc7d97f046cb0c0da1f939b1733bc.SC2Replay

has a max value 1883 of screen_feature/unit_type
I didn't try all the replays, so i don't know how many replays have this phenomenon and what's the exactly max value of screen_feture/unit_type, i also wonder if this will be happen when we run an agent in a full game.
BTW, the replays are all downloaded from [replay files](https://github.com/Blizzard/s2client-proto#downloads)

I found that konwn uint types are defined in pysc2/lib/static_data.py, in which there are 200 types with the largest type id is 894. but according to its reference: https://github.com/Blizzard/s2client-api/blob/master/include/sc2api/sc2_typeenums.h, there are 210 types with the largest type id 1910, none of these numbers is 1850, so what does 1850 stand for? Could anybody explain this, or tell me what's the max type could be, Thanks @tewalds

issue_title: `'geckodriver' executable needs to be in PATH` error in 1.11.2
issue_body:
After update of pytest-selenium to 1.11.2 (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-selenium/commit/0773c93a54f3218d960f4b11b026f2b58a918922 to be more precise) my selenium tests stopped working (using Firefox).
I started to receive `WebDriverException: Message: 'geckodriver' executable needs to be in PATH.` error.
Debugging has shown that `capabilities` now are getting set to `{'acceptInsecureCerts': True, 'browserName': 'firefox', 'marionette': True}`, instead of `{'browserName': 'firefox'}`, which was the case before the update (now it takes them from selenium's `DesiredCapabilities`)

issue_title: fixed variables interactions
issue_body: The `fixed_variables` option of `CMAEvolutionStrategy` interacts with `minstd` and `maxstd` and `integer_variables` options. This leads to an error for the former and probably to unexpected results for the latter. We may consider to depreciate `fixed_variables` in favour of `fitness_transformations.FixVariables`. In this case the values above options would need to be managed by the user.

issue_title: Command for downloading packages-microsoft-prod.deb is defective
issue_body:
On this page: https://www.microsoft.com/net/download/linux-package-manager/ubuntu16-04/runtime-current
there is the command: `wget -q packages-microsoft-prod.deb https://packages.microsoft.com/config/ubuntu/16.04/packages-microsoft-prod.deb` but the command is defective.

Currently it tries to download two files, one from the correct location and another from a URL "packages-microsoft-prod.deb" which obviously is not a valid URL and therefore it quietly fails because of the `-q` flag. The exit code of the command (which is 4 - Network failure) of course indicates that an error has occurred.

The problem is hidden when the command is executed on the command line as it is but when trying to execute it as a RUN command in Dockerfile, as part of building a Docker image, it fails because of the non-zero exit code.

The fix would include the `-O` option, or removing the `packages-microsoft-prod.deb` part. I would propose this as a fix:

`wget -qO packages-microsoft-prod.deb https://packages.microsoft.com/config/ubuntu/16.04/packages-microsoft-prod.deb`

issue_title: Feature request: in-place, upgradeable installers
issue_body: **Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.** Old versions of .NET Core are taking a lot of disk space on my dev machine. This is in part because Visual Studio so rapidly updates and automatically brings in new .NET Core versions. Runtime: I don't need most of them because the runtime will auto-rollforward to latest patched versions. SDK: because the SDK is highly backwards compatible, I generally don't use global.json because the latest stable SDK works well for most projects. **Describe the solution you'd like** I'd like a way to have the Windows installers upgrade in place and trim unused older runtimes and SDKs. I'd also like a way to cleanup unsued packages from `C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\NuGetFallbackFolder`. **Describe alternatives you've considered** * The runtime and SDK installers could use MSI to do the in-place upgrade * Provide a tool such as `dotnet-uninstaller` which can trim unused versions of the runtime/SDK. * Provide a tool to cleanup `C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\NuGetFallbackFolder` to remove packages matching unused, old runtimes **Additional context**

issue_title: Signature verification ignores the Key ID in the signature
issue_body:
### Summary
`keys.verify_signature` takes a **key** and a **signature** (along with the data over which the signature may have been signed). Signature verification determines only whether or not the signature value (`sig`) can have been produced by the provided **key**. It does **not** check whether or not the signature itself reports that it was made using that key, so the keyid listed in the signature provided need not match the key in order for the signature to be deemed valid. Correct behavior, I would say, is to deem the signature invalid if the key id it lists is not the key id that is expected, without bothering to determine if the signature value ('sig') is correct.

### Example
Suppose I have two keys, k1 and k2. I use k1 to sign data, and then change the signature dictionary to claim that I used k2 by listing k2's key id, even though I signed with k1. I send you the signature. You want to know if it was generated by k1. You run `keys.verify_signature` and everything checks out, even though the signature incorrectly claims to be a signature by k2.

### Security Implications
Note that this can mean that an attacker could have a signature deemed valid without knowing what the right keyid is, and that, in upstream code, a single signature can be deemed valid if it could have been generated by any acceptable key, without the attacker having to choose one key or attempt multiple times listing different key ids.

While this may not be a **large** security issue -- the signature value itself must still match a signature produced by the provided key over the provided data -- it's confusing, likely breaks assumptions in upstream code, and has **some** security impact upstream (where you can use the same signature in the same signature verification request to try to match any acceptable key's signature without choosing a single key).


Note that this behavior was encountered in TUF and some upstream issues are implied by that fact. I'll address that in an issue in TUF.

https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/blob/433670b84f9d1490bacfcd2ba67b9ec77df0d970/securesystemslib/keys.py#L940-L946

issue_title: Ambiguous time support for all time zone classes.
issue_body:
Before the 2.6.0 release, all timezones with transitions should support ambiguous times.

See #225 and #248.

The classes with non-fixed offset support are:

- [x] `tzlocal` (PR #248)
- [x] `tzfile` (PR #225)
- [x] `tzwin` (PR #225)
- [x] `tzwinlocal` (PR #225)
- [ ] `tzstr`
- [ ] `tzrange`
- [ ] `tzical`

Right now the implementations are all ad hoc because transitions are determined differently for each class. Ideally, we'd refactor all the time zone classes around a single base class and try and be a bit more systematic about how this is done, but for now I think what we've got is fine.

issue_title: RFC: Develop plan to replace Parsley
issue_body:
We currently use [parsley](http://parsley.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) to parse HGVS expressions. Parsley was last released in 2015. The most recent commit on github is 2017. The project appears to be defunct, which presents a risk for the hgvs package.

Since swapping parser will take significant time and likely create backward-incompatible changes, we should target such a move for a major release (e.g. hgvs 2.x).

https://tomassetti.me/parsing-in-python/ has a great review of Python parser tools.

issue_title: Extension module must be linked with librt on Linux
issue_body:
While trying out vmprof (git clone; python setup.py develop), I've ran into this (Ubuntu 12.04 x86_64):
```
% python -c 'import vmprof'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "vmprof/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    import _vmprof
ImportError: ./_vmprof.so: undefined symbol: clock_gettime
```
...which is due to `clock_gettime` being defined in librt.so

A simple patch
```diff
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ else:
                                'src/vmprof.c',
                                ],
                             extra_compile_args=['-Wno-unused'],
-                            libraries=['elf', 'unwind'],
+                            libraries=['elf', 'unwind', 'rt'],
                             extra_link_args=['%s' % libdwarf])]
```
would fix the issue, though not in a portable way (at least some BSDs have this function defined in libc).

Not a pull request as I haven't found a clean&reliable way to do this yet (autotools folks [just test it](http://trac.mpich.org/projects/mpich/ticket/1013), but distutils does not seem to be flexible enough).

issue_title: Instance fields
issue_body:
Is it possible to have fields specified(defined) by a schema instance(e.g. in a schema's `__init__` method)? 

If not, is there a reason why that shouldn't/couldn't be possible? 

The use case for this is the same as that of any class: to have the class specify attributes that are unchanging accross instances, but to have instances differ among each other on some attributes.

For example, to define a class of schemas for which some fields depend on another schema to be chosen at runtime:

```
class DependentSchema(Schema):
    def __init__(self, dependency, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.dependent_field = dependency.fields["a_field"]

    static_field = fields.Field() 
    class Meta:
        fields = ("static_field", "dependent_field")
```


Thanks.

```python
import sacred

issue_title: Unexpected value change in multiple calls to check-sat
issue_body:
I'm observing something I haven't expected, using a freshly checked out copy of z3.

For this input:

```
(set-logic QF_BV)
(declare-fun s0 () (_ BitVec 8))
(check-sat)
(assert (bvsge s0 #x05))
(assert (bvslt s0 #x07))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

Z3 says:

```
sat
sat
((s0 #x06))
sat
((s0 #x05))
```

Now, there's nothing really wrong here, as z3 is producing a valid value in each case. But I'm surprised to see that the value is changing between the second and the third call to `check-sat`, even though nothing changes in between.

The above script looks "weird" indeed, but it's the minimum I was able to reduce to from a more complicated scenario.

[Furthermore, if I use `Int` for the type (as opposed to `(_ BitVec 8)`), the model remains the same.]

Is this the expected behavior? While there isn't anything that says successive calls to `check-sat` with no assertions in between should produce the same model, I'm surprised to see that that does not happen.

Put slightly differently, I'd have expected two consecutive calls to `check-sat` with no intervening `assert`s in between to produce the precise same model, which doesn't seem to be the case in Z3. Perhaps that's a property it should have?

But there is no easy way to determine which filter on a grid is causing the issue. The exception should include the name of the filter.

issue_title: Add bias to NonlinearityLayer
issue_body:
For building an auto-encoder via #373, it would be useful to be able to drag out not only the nonlinearity, but also the bias from a layer into a separate layer (so the `UnLayer`/`InverseLayer` can be built to not invert the nonlinearity and bias, but be followed by its own bias and nonlinearity).

We could either add a `BiasLayer` for that, or regard it as part of the `NonlinearityLayer`. I think it wouldn't hurt to add it to the latter.

To be useful for different purposes, the bias should broadcast over all but the second dimension by default (that would be reasonable for dense and convolutional layers), but also be customizable to broadcast over a set of given axes (as in my [`BatchNormLayer` implementation](https://gist.github.com/f0k/f1a6bd3c8585c400c190)). This would also partly address the use case mentioned in #249.

issue_title: Python and Django support policy looks not secure
issue_body:
django-money supports this Django and Python versions:
> Django versions supported: 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.8, 1.9, 1.10, 1.11
> 
> Python versions supported: 2.6, 2.7, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6

Supported Django versions (official):
https://www.djangoproject.com/download/ (scroll down to image or table)
Django 1.8 LTS, 1.10, 1.11 LTS

Supported CPython versions:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CPython (look at History table)
2.7, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6

Using unsupported versions may create secure hole in your project. Best support policy for any project - do not support versions of required packages, which are already unsupported by their developers. It will make code clean and prevent of adding secure holes by your product users.

It's good idea to drop support for Python 3.2, Django 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.9 and follow this policy in future.

issue_title: Where do I start?
issue_body:
Where do I start, there are countless issues with this API, let me address some of them here.

First is the need to patch an external module to fix issues with _your_ API, you could bundling that package locally rather than relying on the user to install the package then modify it with your requirements. 

There is no way to specify the location of the configuration file for the Client, it is just hard coded to a non absolute path. 

```
########
##
##  Setup web service connectivity by getting need config data, security tokens etc.
##
########
class ET_Client(object):
```

Okay, style wise, this really needs to be a document string. The bracket of comments look ugly and take up 5 lines. 

It would be far nicer to read

```
class Client(object):
    """Setup web service connectivity by getting needed config data, security tokens etc."""
```

The naming scheme in this project is inconstant at best. The naming of every class in this library is prefixed with `ET_` this is a redundant naming convention and isn't used in Python. Sometimes variables and functions are defined as `self.authToken` and sometimes its `self.refresh_token()` and sometimes its `def determineStack(self):` 
It really doesn't matter which one is used but stick to a style and use it.
##### [ET_Client.py#L59](https://github.com/ExactTarget/FuelSDK-Python/blob/master/ET_Client.py#L59)

You are using an undefined variable before its defined. This will cause horrible errors during execution.
##### [ET_Client.py#L864](https://github.com/ExactTarget/FuelSDK-Python/blob/master/ET_Client.py#L864)

Are those comments or another language? If so why are they presented as nop strings? 
##### [ET_Client.py#L936](https://github.com/ExactTarget/FuelSDK-Python/blob/master/ET_Client.py#L936)

`## FIX THIS`

Comments like this are just scary. At least throw me an undefined behaviour exception so I know something unplanned is happening.
##### [ET_Client.py#L563](https://github.com/ExactTarget/FuelSDK-Python/blob/master/ET_Client.py#L563)

Most scary are your **init** methods. 

```
def __init__(self):
    super
```

Has _no_ effect at all. 
If there is inherited behaviour these classes are relying on this could cause subtle breaks in the object.

When you have used super correctly you forgot to specify the `where` clause.
##### [ET_Client.py#L504](https://github.com/ExactTarget/FuelSDK-Python/blob/master/ET_Client.py#L504)

You  return values from `__init__` in several places. This is going to cause runtime exceptions all over the place.
##### Other issues!

My lint and code smell tools alone detected 180 code issues, most of which were mostly naming and style violations but some of which are more obvious, let me list a few here
- Raising a string.
- Unbound local variables.
- Catch All Exceptions.
- Unused imports.

This is supposed to be a professional project. The code in this client library is horrid. I have spent a whole morning trying to modify it to bring it up to standard and I have given up after the mountain of major issues just kept piling up. 

This code is totally unfit for production until someone really takes a chainsaw to it and cuts away everything that they can.

Can not set customize geotiff tags while using satpy geotiff writer with rasterio method.

It was working with satpy v0.7.4.

**To Reproduce**

```python

#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from satpy import Scene
from satpy.utils import debug_on
from satpy.writers import get_enhanced_image, to_image

from satpy.writers.geotiff import GeoTIFFWriter

import glob

debug_on()

dt_start = "s20180791545447"
    
filenames = glob.glob('./ABI-L1b-RadF/OR_ABI-L1b-RadF-M3C??_G16_' + dt_start + '*.nc')

glb_scn = Scene(reader='abi_l1b', filenames=filenames)

glb_scn.load(['C07'])

img = get_enhanced_image(glb_scn['C07'])

mytags = {'TIFFTAG_IMAGEDESCRIPTION' : 'NDATA_TYPE=mydtype'}
outfile = './test.tif'

gtiff_writer = GeoTIFFWriter(tags=mytags)

img.save(outfile, writer=gtiff_writer)

issue_title: Multiple Ganga sessions
issue_body:
Hi all,

As per #235 I think it's worth bringing up multiple concurrent Ganga sessions.

I would like to propose that if Ganga finds an active session lock that it should by default disable the monitoring thread as well as informing the user. This should prevent some level of repository clashes that we see if someone forgets and launches 2 versions of ganga in parallel.


I think a short message informing the user that they've started in a _read-only_ mode and that they can enable monitoring would be useful if we do this.

I think this also strongly merits that we check that both ```enableMonitoring()``` and ```disableMonitoring()``` do what is advertised.
I've been working on a test for this and have spotted a few minor problems which I'd like to put into a smaller PR in the near future.

Does everyone else think this sounds sane?

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: Doxyfile INPUT too broad
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | N/A | 
| Firmware Version | N/A  | 
| Operating System & Version | Ubuntu 16.04  |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.4.0 |

In the doxy configuration file "doc/doxygen/doxyfile" you have the following configuration: 

```
INPUT                  = ../../include \
                         ../../include/librealsense \
                         ../ \
                         ../../src
```

In the file "doc/readme.md", to build the documentation it says to run: 
```
sudo apt-get install doxygen
doxygen doc/doxygen/doxyfile
```

It is not explicitly stated, but it seems the assumption would be to run this command from the root project directory. If you do this, the INPUT definitions point to the parent directory of the root project directory, bringing in anything found there as well. So if you have other opensource projects in that directory, the doxygen documentation also brings in things that are completely unrelated.

So instead, I ran from the "doc" subdirectory of the project. This seems to correctly find the sources for building the project. The only INPUT definition that actually matches is the '../' definition. Unfortunately, if you have patched the kernel prior to running this command, then the kernel sources will be in subdirectory of the project ("ubuntu-xenial-master" in my case). Doxygen then attempts to build all of the documentation for the entirety of the kernel sources. This quickly caused the small instance I was using to run out of memory. It seems unlikely to me that you would intentionally want to build doxygen for the entirety of the linux kernel.

I think the INPUT definition needs to be changed to avoid building the kernel's docs.

issue_title: Test Incremental export works the same format as normal export
issue_body:
I need to clean this up and break it into tests. Putting it as a place holder.
Allow for new `incremental_export_repomd` exporter distributor config option 

Previously, incremental exports were in a custom format where each erratum and
RPM were written to disk, along with a set of JSON files with unit metadata.
This required special handling of incremental imports that was significantly
different a "regular" repo sync.

A new `incremental_export_repomd` distributor option has been introduced. When this
is set, the incremental repo export generates metadata so that the on-disk
files can be synced via the typical yum sync process. Note that you would not
want to remove old units when doing this, since it would remove any units not
in the incremental :koala:.

This patch also disables the repomd revision optimization if the feed URL is
overriden, which allows the following sequence to work:

sync from upstream URL at midnight
upstream repo gets updated at 1am
sync an incremental at 2am
sync from upstream URL at 3am
Without disabling the optimization, the last sync wouldn't pick up the changes.
Disabling the optimization on feed URL overriding also allows recovery in this
sequence:

sync from upstream URL
sync incremental with `remove_missing` set. Oops!
When the optimization is disabled via feed overide, you can simply re-sync from
the upstream URL to fix this :cat2:.

issue_title: `default_value=` doesn't work with NonNulls
issue_body:
There doesn't appear to be a way to specify default values when using `NonNull` (which, somewhat ironically, seems like the most obvious moment to want a default value.)

The following test shows what I've tried so far:

```
import unittest
import graphene
from collections import OrderedDict

class NonNullDefaultValueTestCase(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_default_value_as_kwarg_to_type(self):
        # This fails with:
        # NonNull could not have a mounted Boolean() as inner type. Try with NonNull(Boolean).
        class User(graphene.ObjectType):
            name = graphene.String()
            is_admin = graphene.NonNull(graphene.Boolean(default_value=False))

    def test_default_as_kwarg_to_NonNull(self):
        # In light of the previous error, I tried this, which also fails, but differently:
        # graphql.error.base.GraphQLError: Cannot return null for non-nullable field User1.isAdmin.
        class User(graphene.ObjectType):
            name = graphene.String()
            is_admin = graphene.NonNull(graphene.Boolean, default_value=False)

        class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
            user = graphene.Field(User)

            def resolve_user(self, *args, **kwargs):
                return User(name="foo")  # I expect the default value to be picked up for is_admin

        schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)
        expected = OrderedDict([('user', OrderedDict([('name', 'foo'), ('isAdmin', False)]))])
        observed = schema.execute("{ user { name isAdmin } }")

        self.assertIs(observed.errors, None)
        self.assertEqual(observed.data, expected)
```

Stepping through it in the debugger, I see that `NonNull` tucks away its `args` and `kwargs` (presumably for later use) but I don't see them ever getting used. Am I missing something here?

issue_title: Tensorboard Summaries in Re-entered Scopes
issue_body:
This issue had been migrated from tensorflow/tensorflow#8387.

> Tensorboard creates a new unique scope for summaries every time existing variable scope is re-entered leading to summaries being split to different groups in the Tensorboard. This might be due to summaries using name scopes internally and re-entered variable scopes having unique `original_name_scope`.
> 
> ```python
> def print_scope(scope):
>     print('               scope.name: {}'.format(scope.name))
>     print('scope.original_name_scope: {}'.format(scope.original_name_scope))
> 
> with tf.variable_scope('parent') as parent_scope:
>     print_scope(parent_scope)
>     with tf.variable_scope('childA') as childA_scope:
>         print_scope(childA_scope)
> print()
> with tf.variable_scope(parent_scope):
>     print_scope(parent_scope)
>     with tf.variable_scope('childB') as childB_scope:
>         print_scope(childB_scope)
> ```
> outputs
> ```
>                scope.name: parent
> scope.original_name_scope: parent/
>                scope.name: parent/childA
> scope.original_name_scope: parent/childA/
> 
>                scope.name: parent
> scope.original_name_scope: parent/
>                scope.name: parent/childB
> scope.original_name_scope: parent_1/childB/
> ```
> Child B is created in re-entered parent scope and has prefix for parent in the `original_name_scope`. I believe parent prefix is what confuses name scope in Tensorboard summaries. I think re-entering existing variable scope should not have these unique prefixes for parent scope.
> 
> I have lemmatizer wrapped in Python class and Tensorboard summaries are created in different stages of graph build with re-entered variable scopes. Tensorboard splits graphs like so: [Screenshot](http://i.imgur.com/KRDt3wj.png)
> 
> Tested on Tensorflow 1.0.1
>

### Description

I'm trying to run `safety check --full-report`. I expect the check to pass, but it fails with a pyyaml vulnerability. Safety has a dependency on dparse, which has a dependency on pyyaml. I'm running safety against a package (no requirements.txt), so it falls back to checking installed packages via pip's API. issue_title: RFC: new driver for Cisco 350 series devices
issue_body:
I've been working on a [driver for Cisco 350 series devices](https://github.com/jasperla/napalm-s350) though I'd like to confirm the name before requesting moving it to [napalm-automation-community](https://github.com/napalm-automation-community).

Now, the datasheet or other forms of documentation are not very clear on which operating system devices run. Based on what's printed when the device boots and inspection of the binary firmware image itself, it seems some form of Linux:

```
Linux MARVELL_LINUX 3.10.70 #2 SMP Sun Aug 20 19:04:38 IDT 2017


MAC Address   :  xx.

Autoboot in 2 seconds - press RETURN or Esc. to abort and enter prom.

*******************************************************************
*** Running  SW  Ver. 2.3.5.63  Date Aug 20 2017  Time 19:03:04 ***
*******************************************************************

HW version is V02
Base Mac address is: xx
Dram size is  : 512M bytes
Dram first block size is  : 370688K bytes
Dram first PTR is  : 0x89600000
Dram second block size is  : 4096K bytes
Dram second PTR is  : 0x19000000
Flash size is: 256M
20-Aug-2017 06:28:08 %CDB-I-LOADCONFIG: Loading running configuration.
20-Aug-2017 06:28:09 %CDB-I-LOADCONFIG: Loading startup configuration.
Device configuration:
Slot 1 - SG350-10
Device 0: GT_98DX3233 (AlleyCat3)
CPLD version is: 0x0e
```

This is quite different from the previous Cisco 300 series which ran a vxworks-based firmware. So I think the best approach is to name the driver according to how upstream references it in the docs: Sx350. Right?

NB, while the interface is not too dissimilar from IOS, it is actually too different in order to make it work with the existing IOS driver without turning it into one big `if sx350/else` construct.

issue_title: Fix slug collision warnings during build
issue_body:
Warnings are generated for talks that share the same slug. 

For example, the same talk given at two different conferences:

* djangocon-2014/videos/how-do-debug-tool-bars-for-web-applications-work.json
* pycon-au-2014/videos/how-do-debug-tool-bars-for-web-applications-work.json

or lightning talks across conferences:

* kiwi-pycon-2009/videos/lightning-talks.json
* djangocon-2014/videos/lightning-talks.json
* djangocon-2015/videos/lightning-talks.json

It is tempting to just give each a unique slug in the data repository, but I see no reason the slug should be unique as long as the conference/slug combination is unique.

The build process should be updated to check conference/slug uniqueness instead of slug uniqueness.

issue_title: ns, hs, softmax in practise
issue_body:
The training options for the `loss` function [currently](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText#full-documentation) supported are ns, hs, softmax, where

- ns, Skpgram negative sampling or SGNS
- hs, Skipgram Hierarchical softmax
- softmax

Among the papers, an interesting and recent explanation of these methods is provided in [Embeddings Learned by Gradient Descent](http://cis.lmu.de/esslli2017/pdf/print,embedgd.pdf).

By the way in the current FastText docs and tutorial there is clear explanation about when using Negative Sampling, Softmax or Hierarchical Softmax for the ML practitioners this would be very worth indeed, considering different problem domains like

1. Binary Classification (e.g. `ham` or `spam`)
2. MultiClass Classification (e.g. `sports`, `nature`, `politics`, etc.) -  _we have multiple labels and we want the model to give us the most likely single label_
3. MultiLabel Classification (e.g. `car+person`) - _we have multiple labels and we want multiple labels per output_

for the output data. Typically for 1) and 3) the `sigmoid` would be used, giving us a probability for every class independently, while a `softmax` layer would be used for 2) to get the probability distribution over the N classes plus an `arg-max()` function to get the best scores.  For large scale labels we would use the `hs` instead to speed up the training as explained in [Text Classification - Scaling things up](https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/supervised-tutorial.html#scaling-things-up)


This in theory (a good explanation is provided in the recent book "_Deep Learning, A Practitioners' Approach, Adam Gibson & Josh Patterson_"), but when working in FastText, which are the best options and which is the right way to handle the output probabilities for those 3 different cases?

A partial recommendation was provided in https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/issues/478#issuecomment-380524396 where the suggested `loss` function for the case 3) is the `softmax` at this time, while it would be possible to use `ns` but not for testing purposes (the test will use a `softmax` anyways).
Could you please provide a more clear docs about these 3 different important cases in text classification
