issue_title: Can we please just have one Lexicon DNS authentication plugin?
issue_body:
*Or: The approach being used for DNS authentication plugins is madness.*

Currently there are ten DNS providers, five of which are using Lexicon.¹ There are eleven issues open for creating Lexicon-backed authenticators for specific DNS providers.²

Lexicon currently supports thirty-five providers—so should there be twenty-odd more tracking issues? And it has requests for almost twenty more, and doubtless more exist that could be added.

Because I’d like to use it, I just went and created a Vultr plugin, simply copying the DNSimple one and making the required changes—which were all trivial save one, just renaming “DNSimple” to “Vultr”, “dnsimple” to “vultr”, updating the URL to instruct the user on where get the API key from, and adjusting the check for suggesting to the user that the API key might be wrong (because DNSimple uses HTTP status 401, while Vultr uses 403). The total diff is +808 −5, having created 18 files and touched 12 more, and if I submit it and it is accepted (which the existence of #4600 suggests it would be) it will definitely add to the maintenance burden. Before I started, I was kind of hoping—though not expecting—that it might be +1 −0, given that it’s just piggybacking on top of Lexicon.

This situation seems quite absurd to me: surely the whole idea of Lexicon should be to have *one* authenticator plugin, Lexicon, and let the config file which is passed in specify all necessary details for whichever provider may be desired?

Only two arguments³ get passed for each authenticator plugin—a file to look in, and a propagation-seconds figure. Currently, usage might be like this:

```sh
certbot certonly \
    --dns-vultr \
    --dns-vultr-credentials ~/.secrets/certbot/vultr.ini \
    --dns-vultr-propagation-seconds 10 \
    -d example.com \
    -d www.example.com
```

With this ~/.secrets/certbot/vultr.ini:

```ini
dns_vultr_token = THIRTYSIXRANDOMCHARACTERSOFKEYPLEASE
```

I suggest it should look rather like this:

```sh
certbot certonly \
    --dns-lexicon \
    --dns-lexicon-credentials ~/.secrets/certbot/vultr.ini \
    --dns-lexicon-propagation-seconds 10 \
    -d example.com \
    -d www.example.com
```

With this ~/.secrets/certbot/vultr.ini (feel free to replace `[vultr]` with `provider = vultr` if the former is inconvenient):

```ini
[vultr]
auth_token = THIRTYSIXRANDOMCHARACTERSOFKEYPLEASE
```

(Observe the name auth_token is from Lexicon, rather than having an unnecessary translation layer in the certbot-dns-* plugins.)

This would make *everyone’s* life better. It’d reduce the maintenance burden on Lexicon-supported DNS authentication plugins down to just the one, clean, neatly specified plugin, rather than a base layer plus five, sixteen, thirty copies of almost the same thing, except for their slight deviations and copy-paste errors and forgotten parity updates. It would also make users of other DNS providers happy because they would now be able to readily obtain wildcard certificates.

#4767 and the issues linked to from there may be relevant here; reducing most of the plugins down to just one might help with decision-making; and it’s worth noting that most of Lexicon’s providers don’t require extra dependencies: only namecheap, route53, softlayer, subreg and transip do at present.

----

¹ Those that are using Lexicon: cloudxns, dnsmadeeasy, dnsimple, luadns and nsone; those that are not: cloudflare, digitalocean, google, rfc2136 and route53.

² #4589, #4590, #4591, #4592, #4594, #4595, #4597, #4598, #4599, #4600 and #5745.

³ certbot-dns-route53 is different in that it doesn’t get passed a file name on the command line, but rather uses environment variables to find it. It’s not using Lexicon.

issue_title: Map creates/uploads invalid polygon fence
issue_body:
Versions used: MavProxy 1.5.7 / Arducoptor 3.5.3

MavProxy's Map's draw fence automatically uses the center of the polygon as the first point (return point) uploaded as a geofence. However, if the polygon is concave, like a long dog leg, the calculated center of the polygon can be outside the bounds of the fence. In the case of Arducoptor, this causes the fence to fail its validity check and silently become non-functional.

issue_title: required build is skipped if outside commit range
issue_body:
chartpress logic for whether to build/push an image currently doesn't check whether that image:tag exists. Instead, it *assumes* it does if `--commit-range` is specified and the last change is outside the range. This can be a faulty assumption in a number of cases, including:

- previous build or publish failed
- image prefix changed
- CI didn't build the relevant commit (e.g. due to aborting superseded builds)

Instead, we should modify the build-triggering condition to include a check for existence of the image:

- if image changed in commit range (current)
- **or** image doesn't exist

Alternately, we could skip the "didn't change" check, and only check for image existence (avoids triggering build if called with the same `--commit-range` twice).

issue_title: Issue with path quote under windows.
issue_body:
SoS uses commands such as `python ${path!q}` to get commands such as

```
python '/path/to/file name with space'
```

It works reasonably well but I just found that 

```
python `c:\script.py'
```
under windows is different from

```
python "c\script.py"
```
and would produce error message like 

```
python: can't open file ''c:\script.py'': [Errno 2] No such file or directory
```

We use this technique extensively so we would have to change a lot of things to make this work. Perhaps changing the `!q` would work.

issue_title: remote files target?
issue_body:
Say I have a large number of large raw files stored on a institutional server that are accessible from the cluster. If I would like to process them remotely using sos, I would write a workflow that handles them directly on the cluster...

The problem is that these files do not exist locally so our workflow would choke with 'non-existent' input file etc. 

Perhaps some sort of

```
input: remote('/path/to/file', host=alias)
```
would help? Say,

1. to get signature, sos would login to remote server and calculate it.
2. to process the file locally, sos would copy the file.
3. to process the file on the host, sos would not copy the file.
4. to process the file on another server, sos would copy from host to that server.

Doable but sounds a bit complicated.

I'd like to implement a hash for each dataset, that is unique enough for our general applications, and short enough for humans to read :)

Reasons for that:
- easily identify a dataset by its hash so that it is independent of folder structure and things like that
- add that (human readable) hash to i.e. a plot or my logbook so that I am always sure that I know where the data was exactly
- eventually in the far future have a way that I in my logbook have a link `qc://notebook/S6N09W65ZL`  which loads a data-analysis notebook

If we think that is useful we'd need to make sure we can generate smart hashes, where it is unlikely that we have a double version. 
We could:
```Python
import random
import string
N = 10
hash = ''.join(random.SystemRandom().choice(string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits) for _ in range(N))
```
I have no idea how unique it is though...

we can of course make a really really long string but thats hard to put in a plot title or something like that.

Any ideas/comments about that?

issue_body:
In PR #1186, an issue is fixed with `pyvisa` method `query_binary_values` from version `1.9.0`. Read more about this [on the pyvisa github, issue 348](https://github.com/pyvisa/pyvisa/issues/348).

Once the new `pyvisa` version `1.9.1` is released with the fix, we need to revert the changes made in PR #1186.

issue_title: Dataset crashes when calling "add_results" if array is too large: "OperationalError: too many SQL variables"
issue_body:
The following code reproduces this error: 

```python
from qcodes import ParamSpec, new_data_set, new_experiment

new_experiment("bug", "buggy")
data_set = new_data_set("none")

x = ParamSpec('x', 'number', label='none', unit='none')
data_set.add_parameter(x)
result = [{"x": v} for v in range(1000)]  # This works with 999
data_set.add_results(result)
```

The issue appears to be in the "insert_many_values" function of the sqlite_base module. The smallest value where this issue appears is 1000. This agrees with the sqlite documentation here: http://www.sqlite.org/limits.html  (see Maximum Number Of Host Parameters In A Single SQL Statement)

A proposed solution is to add results to the database in chunks of sizes 999 or smaller.

issue_title: implementing a ramp
issue_body:
It is possible to implement a ramp using qc.Loop sweeping a parameter and adding a wait time but I think there is some merit to discussing whether this is the way it should be done

`qc.Loop(gate.voltage.sweep(0, -10, 1)).each(qc.Wait(1)).run()`

- It creates a data set which we probably don't want
- imo its not a very natural syntax
- mostly we want to ramp for every set of a parameter and to be able to choose max ramp speed at the start of the experiment and then never worry about it again which seems like something that could go in an instrument driver and then be used in place of the plain 'set' (so no Loop at all)...

What are people's thoughts on this? It seems like something everyone would use, how should it be implemented and what would the ideal syntax be?

@giulioungaretti @WilliamHPNielsen

issue_title: Obtaining the string representation of a single Node
issue_body:
Hi, I spent some time trying to _upgrade_ a script I wrote on top of `slimit` replacing it with `calmjs.parse`, but I failed to find an equivalent of slimit's `Node.to_ecma()` method.

The script in question basically analyzes the arguments passed to every call of particular functions, to extract some info. It uses an `ASTVisitor` ([this](https://bitbucket.org/lele/metapensiero.extjs.desktop/src/master/src/metapensiero/extjs/desktop/scripts/extjs_deps.py#lines-73) class) and when it finds a call to (say) `Ext.define` it extracts some info from the parameters.

I tried several approaches around `calmjs.parse.unparsers.es5` to obtain the complete name of a `FunctionCall` `identifier`, but I always end up with the whole subtree (that is, not just the name, but the whole function, body included) _stringification_...

Can you give me an hint on how can I achieve that?

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Params not getting passed through on POSTs for Course methods
issue_body:
Version: 0.10.0
Python version: 3.7

I'm having issues with methods for Course where the params I'm sending as **kwargs aren't getting processed by Canvas. I believe I'm making the requests correctly, but if not please let me know. So far I'm having problems with:
course.create_course_section()
course.create_discussion_topic() 
course.create_assignment_group()

As far as I can tell, I'm only having problems for methods where the only param is **kwargs.

Example for create_course_section():
```
course = canvas.get_course(957)
course.create_course_section(
    section={
        'course_section[name]': 'Fall 2018 Term 1',
        'course_section[sis_section_id]': 'FL18T1'}
)
```
This results in a generic section getting created with none of the options I'm setting.


Example for create_discussion_topic():
```
course = canvas.get_course(957)
course.create_discussion_topic(
    discussion={
        'title': 'test discussion',
        'message': 'do stuff'
})
```
This results in a generic discussion getting created with none of the options I'm setting.


Example for create_assignment_group():
```
course = canvas.get_course(957)
course.create_assignment_group(
    group={
        'name': 'test group',
        'sis_source_id': 'test_group'
})
```
This results in a generic assignment group getting created with none of the options I'm setting.

I tried to update a course name using one of the pieces of sample code and that worked fine, but it's a PUT request. I haven't had any issues with POST methods that take params in addition to **kwargs, such as create_folder().

Any ideas?

issue_title: figure
len of iterator incorrect for dynamic batching
issue_body:
The `__len__` method of `Iterator` (defined [here](https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data/iterator.py#L157)) returns a wrong result for dynamic batching (i.e. if [batch_size_fn](https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data/iterator.py#L50) is not None).

For example if we set `batch_size_fn` to
```
lambda x, n, b: b + len(x.text)
```
there might be more or fewer batches in the dataset than the `__len__` outputs.

           (value.startswith('http://') or

issue_title: [rllib] custom policy networks (custom weights initializers)
issue_body:
Hi,

For some situations, for example when we use selu activation functions, we need to use custom initializers instead of "typical" xavier.

For observation processing models all look quite simple - we can just define our custom model, but when it comes to policy model ([example](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/agents/ddpg/ddpg_policy_graph.py#L25))....it's a bit more complicated.

So, it looks like good feature to give an opportunity to configure weight initialisation, nevertheless, it's a bit tricky. For now, I see 3 ways to do this:
1. just specify something like `initializers.xavier_initializer(uniform=True, seed=42)` in config and eval it at network creation (hacker way).
2. specify something like:
```
"initializer": {
    "initializer": xavier_initializer,
    "uniform": true,
    "seed": 42
}
```
I use such configuration in my projects.
3. add possibility to use custom policy networks? or just q/value networks?

If you want, I can PR for more Ray-way solution.
For now, I see several files of interest:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/agents/ddpg/ddpg_policy_graph.py
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/models/fcnet.py
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/models/visionnet.py
- and other rllib agents.

PS. I wonder, why do you use `normc_initializer` for `FullyConnectedNetwork`? Is it works better than xavier?
PS2. Why do you use `tf.contrib.layers` instead of `tf.layers`? Are they better of it is just legacy code?

```
   ...:             redirect_output=True,
   ...:             include_webui=False,
   ...:             redirect_worker_output=True,
   ...:             use_raylet=True)
```


### Describe the problem
Reported by @devin-petersohn .
When importing ray with raylet enabled, the user may occasionally get an alarming message that looks like this:
`ray/src/common/io.cc:119: Connection to socket failed for pathname /tmp/raylet23223245`
Despite the message, driver's connection to raylet actually succeeds and the application behaves normally. 
Suggestion to do one of the following:
(a) make the error message sound more like a temporary warning, e.g., "WARN: driver's connection to socket temporarily unsuccessful"
(b) suppress the warning and only print the connection error when all the raylet connection retries failed.

issue_title: Why HStore and not JSON(B)?
issue_body:
Do you think it's reasonable to migrate the library to JSON(B) in a long term?

On one hand using HStore makes a lot of sense as it's the simplest data type available in Postgres that does the job. However, JSONB is *composable*. That is, one can store JSON in a JSONB-backed LocalizedField and retain all the postgres goodness if one decides to drop into raw SQL.

If I understand correctly, all the recent stuff around indexes and uniqueness can be redone on top of JSONB, too.

The only downside I can think of is [a slight performance hit](https://blog.heapanalytics.com/when-to-avoid-jsonb-in-a-postgresql-schema/) when filtering on JSONB fields. However, I don't think this is a reasonable pattern anyway.

What do you think?

issue_title: Better support for writing bytes
issue_body: When writing a FixedString column, the data is first encoded using utf-8 to bytes in Python. This could cause a problem when the string cannot be encoded using utf-8. For instance, cipher text bytes generated by hmac sha256. I would suggest add type checking before encoding in https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/blob/master/src/writer.py#L18 and https://github.com/mymarilyn/clickhouse-driver/blob/master/src/writer.py#L24. If the text is already bytes, just don't encode it. For example, `import hmac; hmac.new(b"done", msg=b"yes", digestmod="sha256").digest().decode('utf-8')` will throw an exception `'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xcb in position 3`.

issue_title: Issues with the spline object in IceSheet
issue_body:
Currently the way IceSheet stores temperature as a spline doesn't make much sense. Specifically it takes the spline as input and also gives it as a diagnostic, when the diagnostic is supposed to be the value at the time of the input state. In the output we also give a profile of temperature, but it's as a 2D array of array objects (a quasi-3D array). This is very non-standard.

Instead we should have a separate vertical dimension for ice sheet level, and input and output arrays with multi-level ice sheet temperatures. Especially because for our object, the number of ice sheet levels is static.

issue_title: [V3] The trivia list repository and namespace packages
issue_body:
Alright, I figure I should write up my thoughts on this topic here to get a proper discussion going.

Currently, for those who are not aware, the trivia lists (YAML files containing question and answers for trivia) are located in the [Red-Trivia](https://github.com/Cog-Creators/Red-Trivia) repo. This is its own (PyPI-hosted) python package, and it is listed as a dependency of Red-DiscordBot. However, in order to make the trivia repo install in the same site-packages folder of a user's python environment (and to keep import names uniform), we install the package inside a **namespace package**, `redbot.ext`. 

Namespace packages have a few rules and there are multiple ways to set them up, the recommended way being to make them just a plain folder (without an `__init__.py`). This is how `redbot.ext` is currently set up, however it is still wrong. Namespace packages *cannot* be beneath normal python packages, in our case the `redbot` package. This is because setuptools will not only declare `redbot.ext` as a namespace package, but also any the parent packages of that name. So, `redbot.ext` and `redbot` get declared as namespace packages.

`redbot` should not be declared as a namespace package, because this means `redbot/__init__.py` will be executed **every time setuptools or pkg_resources is imported from *any application* inside the python envrionment**. Inside `redbot/__init__.py` exists the line `import discord`. This can throw an error in many ways, particularly if there is something incomplete with the python environment's dependencies (perhaps discord.py isn't installed, or one of its sub-dependencies). This is why often whenever we are missing a dependency of some sort, our pip constantly spews meaningless tracebacks and the only solution is to nuke the python environment and start again.

Alright, so they are mostly the issues with our current use of namespace packages (sorry for the wall of text). Now, I wanna talk about solving this.

### Do we need namespace packages?
After all of the annoyance they've been, their unpythonic nature and even a comment from Donald Stufft describing them as "broken" (although I've admittedly taken [this](https://github.com/pypa/python-packaging-user-guide/issues/265#issuecomment-241858136) out of context), I'm leaning towards no. The trivia repo is not worth this hassle. My proposed alternative would simply be to have Red-Trivia just install to a `red_trivia` package totally separate from `redbot`.

### Do we need Trivia in a separate repo?
Yes, I'm opening this question back up again. I wasn't a core developer when the decision was made to separate the lists, and whilst I'm not just trying to uproot far-gone decisions, I do sometimes question if our current setup is worth the hassle. Here are some reasons why:

##### Separate release cycles and versions
I don't see a real advantage in separating the release cycle of the trivia lists. If anything, I see it as a disadvantage because: 
 - When users update the bot, Red-Trivia won't be updated (even if there is a new version) unless Red-DiscordBot pins the latest version. That means every time Red-Trivia does a release, we have to manually bump the version on Red-DiscordBot, or else the vast majority of users will never see updates to Red-Trivia (unless they manually update it). We shouldn't need to manually bump the version of a tiny and internally-developed dependency, especially if it is 99% data and 1% code.
 - More releases is simply more to manage for developers. It's much easier for us to just stick some notes in Red's changelog release to say the trivia lists have been updated.

##### Having its own PyPI package
Red-Trivia is 756KB of YAML and 5 lines of python code, the code which trivially returns the paths to the lists. I don't think this is very deserving of a PyPI package in retrospect. I don't see a problem with simply adding one extra line of code in the trivia cog to get the list of paths to the YAML files, and locate the YAML files somewhere appropriate in the redbot repo.

##### Neglect
I often miss things going on at the trivia repo, or forget about them, because I'm constantly pouring over the issues here on this repo. The separate repo doesn't get much attention and can be neglected at times. This *might* be slowing down progress on trivia - I know it takes a while for PR's to get merged over there sometimes, let alone wait for a new release and for the version pin to be updated here.

#### Locating the lists in this repository
I think `redbot/cogs/trivia/data/` would be an appropriate place to locate them, since we already recommend a `data/` subdirectory for managing bundled data with 3rd party cogs - this is essentially following that convention. The only difference is there is no need for bundled data to be copied here, since it's a core cog which stays where it is and the core lists should be shared across instances. This approach is really simple, keeps everything structured categorically (all core trivia things together) and keeps us sane. Simple is better than complex, and the above essay could be swept aside if we simply put up with some YAML files being kept in this repo.

issue_title: Issue with async iterators
issue_body:
I'm trying to implement an async version of `zip` using the following code, but I'm getting an error when one of the iterators passed to `async_zip` raises a `StopAsyncIteration` before the others are finished. Normally I'd expect that I can catch it using the multierror or a try/except, however, instead I'm getting a `RuntimeError`.

I don't understand why this is happening and it's not clear if this is a bug in my code or in trio.

```py
import trio

class async_zip(object):

    def __init__(self, *largs):
        self.nexts = [obj.__anext__ for obj in largs]

    async def _accumulate(self, f, items, i):
        items[i] = await f()

    def __aiter__(self):
        return self

    async def __anext__(self):
        nexts = self.nexts
        items = [None, ] * len(nexts)
        got_stop = False

        def handle(exc):
            nonlocal got_stop
            if isinstance(exc, StopAsyncIteration):
                got_stop = True
                return None
            else:
                return exc

        with trio.MultiError.catch(handle):
            async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
                for i, f in enumerate(nexts):
                    nursery.start_soon(self._accumulate, f, items, i)

        if got_stop:
            raise StopAsyncIteration
        return items

# an async iterable
class it(object):
    def __init__(self, count):
        self.count = count
        self.val = 0
    def __aiter__(self):
        return self
    async def __anext__(self):
        await trio.sleep(1)
        val = self.val
        if val >= self.count:
            raise StopAsyncIteration
        self.val += 1 
        return val

# test the iterable
async def runner():
    async for val in it(4):
        print('got', val)

# let all iterables finish together
async def zipper():
    async for vals in async_zip(it(4), it(4), it(4), it(4)):
        print('got', vals)

# have one iterable finish early
async def zipper_short():
    async for vals in async_zip(it(4), it(4), it(4), it(2)):
        print('got', vals)

# run them
trio.run(runner)
trio.run(zipper)
trio.run(zipper_short)
```

This prints

issue_title: Better handling of os.fork from inside trio
issue_body:
If you call `os.fork` from inside a trio context, and then try to use trio in both the parent and the child, you are going to have a bad time. `fork` is a very powerful tool, and leaves the process in a strange state: the child is just like the parent, except that threads etc. have disappeared, and they share all fd's (including the self-pipe), etc. This seems essentially unsolvable: if you want to start a new process from trio using fork+exec, that's fine, but anything else is probably not going to work. I guess if you fork and then only execute synchronous code in the child then things might be mostly OK (though you've just leaked a bunch of memory in the child, because it will forever keep copies of all the parent process's data, and copy-on-write won't save you because of Python's well-known issues with GC causing un-sharing).

(See also [bpo-21998](https://bugs.python.org/issue21998), which is asyncio's version of this issue.)

We should at least document this. It would also be good to actually catch it and provide some error, since nobody reads the documentation.

One approach is to record the pid when we enter trio, and then each time we touch trio state, verify that `os.getpid()` still gives the same value. I guess "enter trio" here means any of the `@_public` methods in `trio/_core`, yielding to the event loop, and `TrioToken.run_sync_soon`?

In 3.7 there's `os.register_at_fork`, which could potentially be useful (maybe to reduce overhead?), but I'm not entirely sure how.

issue_title: Pass raw spans to exporters
issue_body:
Currently, span objects are converted to an intermediate json format before being sent to an exporter. As a result, there are some stackdriver specific conversions [being applied](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/span.py#L222) to all spans (such as string truncation) that may not necessarily apply to all export formats.

I am proposing to update the [base.Exporter](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/exporters/base.py#L18) emit and export methods to take a raw [span.Span](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/span.py#L28) object instead of a dict. Not only will this push stackdriver specific details to the stackdriver exporter, but it would also make the development of new exporters easier since there are less layers of conversion.

Happy to make a PR if everyone is ok with the change.

issue_title: CELU as an ONNX operator?
issue_body:
I'm adding CELU ([paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.07483.pdf)) to PyTorch (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/8551). I want to be able to export this operator to ONNX, but I don't know what is the best way to do that.

One option is to add a new operator `Celu` to onnx and PyTorch just export a node named 'Celu'. Another option is to export it as composed by existing ops.

I don't know which is better:

On the one hand, `Elu`, `Selu`, `Relu` are all there in ONNX, although they can be easily composed by existing ops. Which makes it looks better to make `Celu` part of the family.

On the other hand, there are some other operators that is also very common but can be composed by existing ops not in ONNX, such as https://github.com/onnx/onnx/issues/870.

issue_title: Can't create gekko object when server is down
issue_body:
When the default server is down a gekko object can't be created, even if only using the local solve option.

Ex: 
from gekko import*
m = GEKKO()

m.solve(remote = False)

Doesn't work when the default server is down.
But when I add the name of a working server:

from gekko import*
m = GEKKO(server='server_name')

m.solve(remote = False)

Then it works.

issue_title: Document namespace and storage model
Support parsing and formatting PEP 345-style dependency strings
issue_body:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#requires-dist-multiple-use

Not sure whether this is a good idea, but just throwing it out here. This would be useful to interface with other tools e.g. Flit, which uses this style to specify requirements.

The parsing part is probably already supported (because `packaging` is used), but the formatting would need additional implementation.

issue_title: drop api.user.get
issue_body: The obscure user object would just be there to send it to other api functions. Those on the other hand would have to implement calls with user_id _and_ user as an object. This makes the api more complicated without bringing any win. So drop it.

issue_title: TriG Serializer

wwai...@gmail.com, 2010-07-09T15:53:43.000Z

contains a serializer for TriG. I plan to do one for N-Quads as well.

Parsers might hopefully come later.

Comment 1 by gromgull
both trig serialisation/parsing should be easy to extend from the current n3 stuff. In fact - the N3 parser is stolen from cwm - if they support trig, we can probably also do so easily.


Comment 2 by wwai...@gmail.com
The trig serialiser in the file i mentioned reuses N3 serialiser and calls it recursively on graphs in the current conjunctive graph, wrapping them in  { ... }

I agree parsing shouldn't be too difficult.

Hmm - I would add this - but we do not have a parser for TriG, which would be a bit asymmetrical :) 

Do you feel inspired to whip up a parser for us? :)
